docs\keystone_collapse_forensic_consolidated.md:16:**Refuted suspects:** (d) "different text" — strict_verify resolves against `direct_quote`, the SAME text the distiller validates against (refuted); (e) reasoning-starvation — the REDUCE wrote 50/57/709 content tokens, yet 0 survived (refuted). The `PG_DISTILL_DEBUG` dump confirmed: ledger=1 finding, REDUCE raw prose present, but the model emitted `[#ev:f004_000:5430-5539]` (finding_id as evidence_id, on a separate sentence) → filter kept only the bare token, prose dropped.
docs\compliance_templates\soc2_evidence_mapping.md:21:| Deployment modes | Cloud (OpenRouter + external APIs) and Sovereign (air-gapped, local inference) |
docs\clinical_rag_validation_sota_2026_05_26.md:19:**Partially reinventing — and the most expensive missing piece is the cheapest to add.** POLARIS's current regex-validator + inline-atom architecture is a **legitimate generation-time citation (G-Cite)** approach that the 2025 literature recognises as a real design choice — not an anti-pattern. But the *qualitative-negation fabrication* that Codex caught ("X did not lead to Y" when evidence shows Y at 0.2-0.4%) is precisely the failure mode that **NLI-based entailment models catch and pure regex cannot** [^nli_review_2025]. Anthropic's Citations API (Jan 2025 GA) **solves the citation-emission problem we're hand-rolling but is Claude-only at generation time, which violates POLARIS's V4-Pro lock and likely violates sovereignty unless Claude is post-validation only** [^anthropic_citations]. The 2025 literature explicitly recommends **"P-Cite-first" (post-hoc) for high-stakes applications, reserving G-Cite for precision-critical settings such as strict claim verification** [^gcite_pcite_2025] — POLARIS's current architecture is in the *minority* of academic guidance, but is not wrong.
docs\clinical_rag_validation_sota_2026_05_26.md:49:**Models.** All Claude active models except Haiku 3. Supports Opus 4.7 (the model this report is running on). Available on Anthropic API and Google Cloud Vertex AI.
docs\clinical_rag_validation_sota_2026_05_26.md:99:**What it does.** Three-way classification (entailment / contradiction / neutral) of `(premise, hypothesis)` pairs using encoder models fine-tuned on MNLI + RAG-specific data. The 2025 SDP paper ("Coarse-Grained Hallucination Detection via NLI Fine-Tuning") shows that **simple fine-tuning of NLI-adapted encoder models on task data outperforms more elaborate feature-based pipelines** [^nli_review_2025]. DeBERTa-v3-large-mnli is specifically recommended as a **cheap, deterministic option well-suited for inline gating in production systems** [^nli_review_2025].
docs\clinical_rag_validation_sota_2026_05_26.md:101:**Negation handling.** **Mixed.** The arxiv 2510.20375 paper "The Impact of Negated Text on Hallucination" shows LLMs broadly struggle with negation, but NLI-style entailment models — by virtue of being trained on MNLI's contradiction class — have a structural advantage: a "did not occur" hypothesis paired with "occurred at 0.4%" premise is exactly the contradiction-class training signal [^negation_paper_2025]. HHEM-2.1 is itself an NLI-derived model trained on RAG-specific data and inherits this advantage (see §3.2).
docs\clinical_rag_validation_sota_2026_05_26.md:105:**Sovereignty fit.** **Perfect.** Encoder models, self-hostable, no vendor lock-in.
docs\clinical_rag_validation_sota_2026_05_26.md:115:**MedScore (the medical-specific decomposition).** Specifically designed for medical free-form text. Achieves **74.4% valid-claim rate on AskDocsAI vs FActScore's 17%**, reduces unverifiable claims from 37.3% to 9.3% [^medscore_2025]. **However: it uses closed-source models (GPT-4o, GPT-4o-mini) for decomposition** — sovereignty incompatible. Could be re-implemented with a local Qwen3 / DeepSeek-distilled judge.
docs\clinical_rag_validation_sota_2026_05_26.md:123:**MedHallu (Feb 2025).** 10k QA pairs derived from PubMedQA with systematically generated hallucinations [^medhallu_2025]. Four hallucination categories: misinterpretation, incomplete information, mechanism misattribution, evidence fabrication. **Hardest to detect: Incomplete Information at 54% accuracy.** Key finding: **adding an "abstention / not sure" option enhances precision by up to 15% for larger models; GPT-4o achieves 79.5% F1 with this feature.** Knowledge provision (give the LLM medical reference text) improves general LLM F1 from 0.533 to 0.784 (+25.1 points) [^medhallu_2025].
docs\clinical_rag_validation_sota_2026_05_26.md:131:**HealthContradict (Dec 2025).** Specifically evaluates biomedical knowledge conflicts in language models [^health_contradict]. Newer and directly relevant — the constipation fabrication is a knowledge-conflict case.
docs\clinical_rag_validation_sota_2026_05_26.md:155:**Perplexity (Sonar models + Deep Research).** Real-time web grounding, cites 50 sources per Deep Research report, achieves **93.9% on SimpleQA, 21.1% on Humanity's Last Exam** [^perplexity_2026]. **Critically: Perplexity does NOT independently fact-check the sources it cites — it relies entirely on source credibility, propagating bias from top search results into output** [^perplexity_2026]. Citation hallucination rate per the Columbia Journalism Review audit: 37% (lowest among major search platforms — but still >1 in 3 citations potentially fabricated or misdirected) [^perplexity_2026]. **This is the key competitive insight: Perplexity is fast and source-rich, but its faithfulness layer is weaker than POLARIS's atom-grounded design when POLARIS is working correctly.**
docs\clinical_rag_validation_sota_2026_05_26.md:204:4. **Issue: I-rag-004 — V4 Pro abstention re-tuning** (0.5 day). Per MedHallu finding (+15% precision with abstention option for large models), add explicit "Do not answer if you cannot cite an atom — emit `[REFUSED:no_atom]` marker" instruction to V4 Pro system prompt. Already partially in place, tighten the wording.
docs\clinical_rag_validation_sota_2026_05_26.md:316:- [Suprmind: Perplexity AI 2026 models, features, citation accuracy](https://suprmind.ai/hub/perplexity/) — 93.9% SimpleQA, 37% CJR citation hallucination

487:    # to ~1000 URLs but left PG_LIVE_MAX_EV_TO_GEN at its code default 20 (run_honest_sweep_r3.py:4524) —
488:    # so generation saw 20 of 1000+ rows (98% silently dropped), the same silent-throttle class as
489:    # I-cap-005 one stage downstream. The interim raise to 150 still dropped ~90% of a 1500-row pool.
490:    # OPERATOR DECISION 2026-06-10: this is the GLOBAL POOL the sections draw from, NOT a per-prompt size
491:    # — each section independently selects its own relevant rows (capped by PG_MAX_EV_PER_SECTION below),
492:    # so a global pool cap only STARVES niche sections of evidence ranked below the cut. There is no
493:    # provider/transport reason for it: the generator (deepseek-v4-pro) is a 1M-context model and a single
494:    # section prompt carries only PG_MAX_EV_PER_SECTION rows. So the pool cap is set to the FULL extracted
495:    # set (= PG_MAX_EVIDENCE_TO_EXTRACT) — no pre-section throttle; nothing is dropped before the sections
496:    # pick. This does NOT enlarge any single LLM prompt (no lost-in-the-middle risk); it only lets each
497:    # section choose its best rows from the full universe. FLOOR semantics (max(existing, 1500)).
498:    #

535:    # "judge_error:..." sentinel) when PG_STRICT_VERIFY_ENTAILMENT=enforce. Force it on so the benchmark's

542:    # Run-level guard: abort if the judge_error RATE across delivered sentences exceeds this (the verifier
543:    # was so degraded the run is not trustworthy). 0.10 = 10%. Surfaced to the manifest either way.
544:    "PG_MAX_JUDGE_ERROR_RATE": "0.10",
545:    # F03 (A3): verified-section-FRACTION floor. The success gate aborts at ZERO verified sections
546:    # (abort_no_verified_sections), but a report where most sections are gap stubs (only a couple
547:    # verify) previously shipped GREEN — a mostly-gap clinical report misrepresented as complete.
548:    # This is a COVERAGE-HONESTY floor (a sibling of the §9.2 per-section "≥40% sentences verified"
549:    # gate, lifted to the SECTION granularity): below it, run_one_query emits the NON-`partial`
550:    # abort_excessive_gap and Gate-B fails the run (query_status_ok). It is NOT a §-1.3 breadth

552:    # the §9.2 0.40 per-unit honesty threshold at section granularity (grounded, not tuned). FLOAT in

tests\fixtures\subagent_prompts\section_writer_response.txt:1:Building on the clinical rationale for non-pharmacological glycemic strategies, this section examines how intermittent fasting (IF) modifies the metabolic pathways most relevant to type 2 diabetes mellitus (T2DM), drawing on weighted and standardized mean differences from systematic reviews that aggregate over 50 clinical studies comparing IF protocols against caloric restriction and usual-diet controls [CITE:ev_12e3ca8d07d9d3ed].
tests\fixtures\subagent_prompts\section_writer_response.txt:5:Weight reduction is the most consistently observed outcome across IF trials, and it is mechanistically central to the improvement of insulin resistance in T2DM. In adults with overweight or obesity, IF reduced fat mass by a mean difference of −0.72 kg compared with a non-intervention diet [CITE:ev_243de27dc515c169]. When IF is combined with structured physical exercise, the weight reduction relative to a control diet plus exercise reaches a weighted mean difference of −1.83 kg (P = 0.001) [CITE:ev_749f00cb5c5eea28], suggesting an additive effect when caloric timing is paired with energy expenditure. The largest effect size is observed with modified alternate-day fasting, which produced a weight reduction of −5.18 kg (95% CI: −7.04 to −3.32) compared with a usual diet, supported by high certainty of evidence [CITE:ev_e7bdb4797d3f6f0f]. The differential between these protocols is clinically meaningful: a five-kilogram loss falls within the range associated with measurable improvements in HbA1c and insulin sensitivity, whereas sub-kilogram fat-mass reductions, although statistically significant, may yield more modest glycemic benefits.
tests\fixtures\subagent_prompts\section_writer_response.txt:28:The table makes the protocol-dependent gradient explicit. Modified alternate-day fasting produces the most pronounced anthropometric and hemodynamic shifts, while time-restricted feeding and other less-restrictive variants yield smaller but statistically robust standardized effects. For T2DM patients, this gradient implies that protocol selection is a meaningful clinical lever rather than a stylistic choice.
tests\fixtures\subagent_prompts\section_writer_response.txt:32:Blood pressure reduction is a parallel mechanism through which IF reduces overall cardiometabolic burden in T2DM. Systolic blood pressure fell by a weighted mean difference of −2.99 mm Hg (P = 0.003) when IF was combined with exercise versus control diet plus exercise [CITE:ev_1ece74de7d460b0e]. The effect is substantially larger under modified alternate-day fasting, where systolic blood pressure decreased by −7.24 mmHg (95% CI: −11.90 to −2.58) compared with usual diet, again with high certainty of evidence [CITE:ev_47ea3711836ebb90]. Reductions of this magnitude in systolic pressure are clinically relevant for T2DM patients, who carry elevated baseline cardiovascular risk.
tests\fixtures\subagent_prompts\section_writer_response.txt:36:The breadth of evidence — drawn from syntheses of more than 50 clinical studies comparing alternate-day fasting and time-restricted feeding with caloric restriction [CITE:ev_12e3ca8d07d9d3ed] — supports a coherent mechanistic picture: IF acts on T2DM through interlocking pathways of weight loss, abdominal fat redistribution, lipid modulation, and blood pressure reduction. The magnitude of these effects scales with protocol intensity, and the addition of exercise produces incremental benefit across multiple endpoints.
tests\fixtures\subagent_prompts\section_writer_response.txt:43:- Modified alternate-day fasting reduced systolic blood pressure by −7.24 mmHg (95% CI: −11.90 to −2.58) compared with usual diet [CITE:ev_47ea3711836ebb90], a clinically meaningful change for T2DM patients with elevated cardiovascular risk.

0.94.[1] Colorectal cancer incidence was the outcome of interest in all included cohorts.[1] The more than 1.7 million participants contributed to the statistical power of the analysis.[1]\n\n### Pathogenic bacteria and their genotoxic metabolites in colorectal carcinogenesis\n\nPathogen: Escherichia coli.[3] Mechanism: alkylate DNA on adenine residues4,5 and induces double-strand breaks.[3] Mutational signature: distinct mutational signature.[3] Toxic metabolite or virulence factor: colibactin.[3] This bacterium produces the genotoxic metabolite colibactin.[3] Colibactin is a virulence factor that damages host DNA.[3] The mechanism of colibactin's genotoxicity involves DNA alkylation.[3] Specifically, colibactin alkylates DNA on adenine residues4,5.[3] Following alkylation, colibactin induces double-strand breaks.[3] The induction of double-strand breaks is a direct outcome of colibactin's activity.[3] As a result of this DNA damage, a distinct mutational signature is generated.[3] This mutational signature is characteristic of colibactin exposure.[3] The distinct mutational signature can be detected in colorectal carcinogenesis.[3] Colibactin is a genotoxin that targets DNA.[3] The genotoxic metabolite colibactin is directly responsible for the DNA damage.[3] Ultimately, the mechanism of colibactin involves alkylating DNA on adenine residues4,5 and inducing double-strand breaks, leaving a distinct mutational signature.[3]\n\n### Probiotic contraindications and at-risk populations\n\nContract-bound content for probiotic_immunocompromised_contraindication did not survive strict verification against retrieved primary source text; this slot is a curator-actionable gap. See manifest.frame_coverage_report and human_gap_tasks.json for per-entity detail.[4]\n\n### Safety\n\nAmong 125 patients with nonblood *Saccharomyces* culture findings, 24 (19%) had used the probiotic, versus 2% (3/123) of controls (OR 10, 95% CI 3–32).[4] Consequently, *S. cerevisiae* var. *boulardii* probiotics are not recommended for patients with indwelling catheters, those who are immunocompromised, or the critically ill.[4] In one trial, a multispecies probiotic preparation administered to patients with severe acute pancreatitis was associated with increased mortality. *Escherichia coli* carrying the pks pathogenicity island produces colibactin, a genotoxin that alkylates DNA on adenine residues and induces double-strand breaks.[4].[3] Cyclomodulin-producing *E. coli* were present in biopsies from 70% of colorectal cancer patients compared with only 3% of healthy controls.[5] Lipopolysaccharide (LPS) from Gram-negative bacteria such as *Fusobacterium* and *Bacteroides* activates NF-κB signaling, driving inflammation and colorectal cancer metastasis.[6].[7] A retrospective registry study across five Finnish university hospitals from 2009 to 2018 identified 46 patients with *Saccharomyces* fungemia; at least 20 (43%) were using *Saccharomyces boulardii* probiotic, and compared with a control group with bacteremia or candidemia, the odds ratio for use of an *S. boulardii* probiotic was 14 (95% CI 4–44).[4] Human intestinal organoids were exposed to genotoxic pks+ *E. coli* by repeated luminal injection over five months; whole-genome sequencing of clonal organoids before and after this exposure revealed a distinct mutational signature that was absent from organoids injected with isogenic pks-mutant bacteria.[3] The same mutational signature was detected in a subset of 5,876 human cancer genomes from two independent cohorts, predominantly in colorectal cancer.[3] Butyrate, a short-chain fatty acid, stimulates proliferation at low concentrations but suppresses it at high concentrations.[6]\n\n### Dose Response\n\nFor total dietary fiber, each 10 g/day increment was associated with a summary relative risk (RR) of 0.90 (95% CI 0.86 to 0.94) across 16 studies including 14,514 cases among 1,985,552 participants, with no significant heterogeneity (I²=0%).[1] In contrast, fruit fiber intake was not significantly associated with CRC risk (RR 0.93, 95% CI 0.82 to 1.05 per 10 g/day), and vegetable fiber showed no association (RR 0.98, 95% CI 0.91 to 1.06 per 10 g/day).[1] Similarly, a review of harnessing gut microbiota for CRC therapy highlights the potential of microbiome modulators but emphasizes the need for rigorous validation and biomarker development before clinical translation.[8] A systematic review and meta-analysis of 25 prospective studies examined the association between dietary fiber and whole grain intake and colorectal cancer (CRC) risk[1] Cereal fiber demonstrated a similar protective dose-response: each 10 g/day increase yielded an RR of 0.90 (0.83 to 0.97) based on 8 studies with 9,487 cases among 1,471,756 participants.[1] Whole grain intake also exhibited a significant inverse dose-response: an increment of three servings daily (90 g/day) was associated with an RR of 0.83 (0.78 to 0.89) across 6 studies with 7,941 cases among 774,806 participants.[1]\n\n### Long-term Outcomes\n\nA meta-analysis across solid tumors indicated that combining FMT with immune checkpoint inhibitors improved pooled objective response rates to 43.1% compared to 36.9% with immunotherapy alone.[9] Fecal microbiota transplantation (FMT) has shown potential to augment therapies for colorectal cancer.[9] In a study, patients with refractory, MSS metastatic CRC receiving tislelizumab and fruquintinib achieved a median progression-free survival of 9.6 months and median overall survival of 13.7 months, with an objective response rate of 20% and a disease control rate of 95%.[9] Probiotic modulation with CBM588 (Clostridium butyricum) combined with cabozantinib and nivolumab significantly increased response rates (74% vs 20%, P=0.01) in a trial of metastatic renal cell carcinoma.[9]\n\n### Limitations\n\nLimitations: The evidence base is heavily skewed, with only 17% of sources classified as T1 primary studies and 62% falling into an unknown tier, which undermines the reliability of the evidence hierarchy. high internal consistency (Cronbach's alpha = 0.79–0.93 across parts) and strong correlation with the original UPDRS (ρ = 0.96), providing a comprehensive framework for tracking these changes across four parts: non-motor experiences of daily living, motor experiences of daily living, motor examination, and motor complications.[3]\n- **Safety.** The FDA labeling for carbidopa-levodopa explicitly warns that patients should be observed carefully when the dosage is reduced abruptly or discontinued, especially if they are receiving neuroleptics.[6]\n- **Population Subgroups.** The choice between rechargeable and non‑rechargeable IPGs was significantly associated with affordability (χ²=19.13, p<0.001), and remote programming capability influenced brand selection.[10]\n\n### Efficacy\n\nPopulation: patients with advanced Parkinson's disease and severe motor symptoms.[1] Intervention: this treatment plus medication.[1] Comparator: medication alone.[1] Primary endpoint: the changes from baseline to six months in the quality of life, as assessed by the Parkinson's Disease Questionnaire (PDQ-39), and the severity of symptoms without medication, according to the Unified Parkinson's Disease Rating Scale, part III (UPDRS-III).[1] Timepoint: six months.[1] Study design: randomized-pairs trial.[1]\n\n### Mechanism\n\nComplication type: hardware complications.[2] The Movement Disorder Society-sponsored Unified Parkinson's Disease Rating Scale, known as the MDS-UPDRS, structures the clinical assessment of Parkinson's disease into four distinct and complementary parts.[3] Whereas Part I addresses the non-motor domain, Part II shifts the focus to motor aspects of daily function.[3] However, Part IV specifically records motor complications, distinguishing them from the core motor examination.[3] The combined clinimetric results of this study support the validity of the MDS-UPDRS for rating PD.[3] The primary source identifies hardware complications as a distinct category of DBS-related adverse events.[2] In contrast, the source does not offer guidance on daily life adjustments or support strategies for patients experiencing hardware complications.[2]\n\n### DBS device contraindications and dopaminergic-withdrawal dosing safety\n\nContract-bound content for dbs_device_mri_safety did not survive strict verification against retrieved primary source text; this slot is a curator-actionable gap. See manifest.frame_coverage_report and human_gap_tasks.json for per-entity detail.[4]\n\n### Long-term Outcomes\n\nThe Movement Disorder Society-sponsored revision of the Unified Parkinson's Disease Rating Scale (MDS-UPDRS), validated in a study of 877 patients from 39 sites, demonstrated high internal consistency (Cronbach's alpha = 0.79–0.93 across parts) and strong correlation with the original UPDRS (ρ = 0.96), providing a comprehensive framework for tracking these changes across four parts: non-motor experiences of daily living, motor experiences of daily living, motor examination, and motor complications.[3] In advanced stages, symptoms are severe and daily life usually requires full-time support.[5]\n\n### Safety\n\nThe FDA labeling for carbidopa-levodopa explicitly warns that patients should be observed carefully when the dosage is reduced abruptly or discontinued, especially if they are receiving neuroleptics.[6] DBS hardware complications are relatively common; in a prospective series of 81 consecutive patients with PD undergoing subthalamic nucleus (STN) DBS followed for a mean of 17 months, hardware complications occurred in 26.2% of patients (atom_009).[2] Specific complications included infections requiring system removal in 2.5% (atom_010) and implantable pulse generator (IPG) removal in 3.7% (atom_011), misplaced leads in 12.5% (atom_012), and lead migration, fracture, or IPG malfunction.[2] A review of the FDA MAUDE database over 10 years identified 221 unique adverse events, with infection being the most common (16.2%) (atom_013) and lead migration accounting for 8.6% (atom_014).[7] Over a third (40%) of all devices with adverse events required return to the operating room for explant or revision (atom_003, atom_004).[7] A review of postoperative psychosis after DBS reported a lifetime incidence of psychosis of 28.3% (atom_018) in PD patients following DBS, underscoring the need for vigilance.[8] Additionally, implantable pulse generator expiration can precipitate a movement disorder emergency with severe parkinsonian worsening or akinetic crisis, necessitating urgent medical evaluation.[9] Abrupt reduction or discontinuation of carbidopa-levodopa has been associated with a symptom complex resembling neuroleptic malignant syndrome (NMS), characterized by hyperthermia, muscle rigidity, altered consciousness, and autonomic dysfunction.[6] Psychosis can emerge after STN DBS, as illustrated by clinical cases where patients developed paranoid ideation, hallucinations, and delusions within months of surgery, requiring antipsychotic treatment.[8] Patients taking dopaminergic medications may experience intense urges to gamble, increased sexual urges, binge eating, and other compulsive behaviors, which may resolve with dose reduction or discontinuation[6]\n\n### Population Subgroups\n\nThe choice between rechargeable and non‑rechargeable IPGs was significantly associated with affordability (χ²=19.13, p<0.001), and remote programming capability influenced brand selection.[10] Despite this, a preliminary survey of 17 DBS patients (85% of those approached) found that 76.5% used their patient programmer once per month or less, 17.7% never used it, and the most common function was checking battery life (58.8%).[11] Adaptive DBS shows promise, as one study reported that overall well‑being significantly improved with adaptive stimulation (p=0.007).[12] Caregivers of intermediate PD spent 6.6 hours per day caring, compared to 3.2 hours for early PD, and were less likely to be employed (25.3% vs 42.4%).[13] Personalized DBS programming using machine learning to optimize gait has produced meaningful improvements in walking, addressing a common post‑DBS challenge.[14] Remote patient monitoring devices led 79.9% of participants to agree that they improved mobility, and 48% perceived themselves as safer.[15] However, a meta‑synthesis of wearable device experiences identified barriers including discomfort, emotional distress, and interface difficulties that hinder long‑term adherence.[16] 87.3% of DBS patients used rechargeable IPGs (r-IPGs), 87.7% were satisfied with their device; among those with r-IPGs, 40.6% felt confident handling the device within one week of discharge, 71.4% recharged independently, and the mean recharge interval was 4.3 days.[10] Caregivers of people with advanced PD had significantly higher perceived burden (ZBI 34.5 vs 24.7 for early PD) and were more likely to take medication due to caregiving (34.7% vs 13.8%).[13] Assistive technologies also contribute: a scoping review reported that home automation significantly increased quality of life (p<0.001), and 63.5% of participants used speech‑to‑text functions to cope with tremors.[15]\n\n### Limitations\n\nLimitations: The pipeline's tier distribution reveals a heavy reliance on lower-quality evidence, with only 10% of sources classified as T1 primary studies and a striking 70% falling into the UNKNOWN tier. Six high-severity contradictions were detected, including a 972.4% relative difference in mortality estimates and a 481.0% divergence in hazard ratio, indicating sources disagree sharply on both magnitude and direction of key outcomes. The evidence horizon spans from 2010-01-01 to the present, but the predominance of unclassified sources and sparse representation in tiers T2 (1%), T3 (1%), and T5 (0%) further limit confidence in the synthesized findings.\n\n## Methods\nPre-registered protocol.json (SHA-256 36a59dc009aa949f...).\nCorpus: Serper + Semantic Scholar + OpenAlex live retrieval, augmented by domain backends (clinical: domain_backends(clinical): {'europe_pmc': 27}).\nRetrieval fetch outcome: 478 of 740 candidate sources fetched; 262 failed or timed out.\nGenerator model: deepseek/deepseek-v4-pro (multi-section: outline + 6 parallel sections + strict_verify + regen-on-failure).\nEvaluator model: google/gemma-4-31b-it (different family).\nSources classified using T1-T7 tier taxonomy.\nInclusion / exclusion per clinical template. Sponsor / conflict-of-interest review per source.\nPrompt-injection sanitization enabled. Retrieved 2026-06-10.\nExpected tier distribution: T1 30-60%, T2 15-40%, T3 5-25%, T4 0-20%, T5 0-15%, T6 0-10%, T7 0-10%. Actual distribution: T1=7%, T2=1%, T3=1%, T4=5%, T5=0%, T6=7%, T7=5%, UNKNOWN=73%.\nCorpus adequacy: decision=proceed, 8/8 thresholds met.\nCompleteness checklist: 7/7 topics covered.\n\n## Capability disclosures\nQuantified trade-off analysis was ENABLED but did not contribute to this report (no verified quantified output); 1905 sourced numbers were extracted but not modeled into a verified quantified comparison.\n\n## Contradiction disclosures\nThe contradiction detector flagged 6 numeric disagreements across the evidence pool. Most are extraction artifacts produced by grouping different measured endpoints, units, sub-populations, time windows, or comparators under the same subject/predicate label. The detector does NOT adjudicate by endpoint, population, timepoint, or source tier; raw detector output is available in `contradictions.json`. Per-flag enumeration (PT08 disclosure):\n\n- unknown / accuracy: cited values range 88.0 to 98.2 % (source tiers: T1, T2).\n- unknown / hazard ratio: cited values range 10.0 to 58.1 % (source tiers: T1, T4).\n- unknown / intracranial hemorrhage: cited values range 4.6 to 16.3 % (source tiers: UNKNOWN).\n- unknown / mortality: cited values range 6.49 to 69.6 % (source tiers: T4, UNKNOWN, T1).\n- unknown / relative risk: cited values range 20.0 to 98.1 % (source tiers: UNKNOWN, T1, T4).\n- unknown / ttr: cited values range 0.8 to 95.0 % (source tiers: UNKNOWN, T4, T1).\n\nClaims made in the body of this report are individually bound to their cited evidence IDs via the strict-verify gate, so the reader can trace any specific numeric discrepancy to its source regardless of detector granularity.\n\n## Qualitative safety-conflict disclosures\nThe qualitative detector flagged 0 present-vs-absent clinical-safety conflict(s) (contraindication / drug-interaction / eligibility / warning / adverse-event causation) and 1343 review-flagged item(s) requiring human adjudication. Status is shown as asserted PRESENT/ABSENT/INDETERMINATE, not a numeric value; review flags are NOT adjudicated conflicts.\n\n- 1343 review-flagged item(s) collapsed to keep the report readable; full per-flag rows are in the `contradictions.json` sidecar (filter `type=\"qualitative\"`, `severity=\"review\"`).\n\n\n## Bibliography\n[1] A Randomized Trial of Deep-Brain Stimulation for Parkinson's Disease — https://www.nejm.org/doi/pdf/10.1056/NEJMoa060281?articleTools=true (tier T1)\n[2] Surgical and hardware complications of subthalamic stimulation —  (tier T1)\n[3] Movement Disorder Society‐sponsored revision of the Unified Parkinson's Disease Rating Scale (MDS‐UPDRS): Scale presentation and clinimetric testing results — https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/pdfdirect/10.1002/mds.22340 (tier T1)\n[4] FDA FDA DBS device labeling — MRI / diathermy contraindications and precautions — https://www.accessdata.fda.gov/cdrh_docs/pdf/P960009.pdf (tier T1)\n[5] End-Stage Parkinson's Disease: Symptoms, Care, and Support — https://mytomorrows.com/blog/patients/end-stage-parkinsons-disease-symptoms-care-and-support/ (tier UNKNOWN)\n[6] FDA FDA carbidopa-levodopa labeling — abrupt-withdrawal hyperpyrexia warning — https://www.accessdata.fda.gov/drugsatfda_docs/label/2020/017555s072lbl.pdf (tier T1)\n[7] Characterizing Complications of Deep Brain Stimulation Devices for ... — https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8269991/ (tier T4)\n[8] Psychotic Symptoms Following Subthalamic DBS in Parkinson's Disease: Three Clinical Cases and Literature Review. — https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC13158162/ (tier T4)\n[9] Challenges in PD Patient Management After DBS - src\polaris_graph\llm\widening_prompt_candidates.py:25:# the same: when the SENTENCE generalizes a SPECIFIC entity / strain / drug member / subgroup /
src\polaris_graph\llm\widening_prompt_candidates.py:26:# population / mechanism / setting in the SPAN to a BROADER class, that is specific->general = NEUTRAL
src\polaris_graph\llm\widening_prompt_candidates.py:27:# (NOT entailed). The opposite direction (span general, sentence a faithful subset) stays ENTAILED.
src\polaris_graph\llm\widening_prompt_candidates.py:33:- ENTAILED: every factual assertion in the SENTENCE is supported by the SPAN. Conservative paraphrase is allowed, and a faithful SUBSET of the span (general span -> narrower true sentence, dropping a CI bound) is still ENTAILED.
src\polaris_graph\llm\widening_prompt_candidates.py:34:- NEUTRAL: the SENTENCE introduces a fact, entity, mechanism, or specificity NOT present in the SPAN (e.g. SPAN says "GLP-1 RAs", SENTENCE says "semaglutide"; SPAN says "adipocyte metabolism", SENTENCE adds "lipid metabolism"). ALSO NEUTRAL when the SENTENCE GENERALIZES a SPECIFIC entity/strain/drug/subgroup/population/setting in the SPAN to a BROADER class (specific->general is NOT entailed: SPAN "S. boulardii not recommended in immunocompromised", SENTENCE "probiotics are not recommended" is NEUTRAL).
src\polaris_graph\llm\widening_prompt_candidates.py:51:First apply the SCOPE-DIRECTION test: if the SENTENCE is BROADER than the SPAN — generalizing a specific entity, drug, strain, subgroup, population, mechanism, or study setting to a wider class — the SPAN does NOT entail it. Only a sentence whose scope is EQUAL TO or NARROWER THAN the span (and otherwise faithful) can be ENTAILED.
src\polaris_graph\llm\widening_prompt_candidates.py:73:1. SCOPE: Is the SENTENCE's claimed entity/drug/strain/subgroup/population/mechanism/setting the SAME AS or NARROWER THAN the SPAN's? src\polaris_graph\generator\multi_section_generator.py:1053:- **M-40: Mechanism section is the narrative-depth lever.** When AT LEAST 3 evidence rows in the summary above contain mechanism-of-action vocabulary — in either the `title:` field or the statement body — you MUST include "Mechanism" as one of the outline sections (5 by default, 6 when regulatory evidence is also present per M-41a above). Trigger vocabulary (any of, case-insensitive): "mechanism", "pharmacokinetic", "pharmacodynamic", "receptor", "half-life", "bioavailability", "metabolism", "agonist", "antagonist", "binding", "signaling", "pathway", "kinetic". A research-grade synthesis explains WHY the intervention works, not only WHETHER it works. Top-tier Deep Research outputs (GPT-5.4 DR, Gemini 3.1 Pro DR) dedicate a full section to mechanism/pharmacology for any clinical efficacy question; a report without it reads as a short brief rather than a deep synthesis. This rule is generalizable: in materials/chemistry a Mechanism section covers reaction pathway / phase transition / interface chemistry; in policy it covers causal pathway / incentive mechanism / enforcement mechanism; in finance it covers transmission channel / market microstructure.
src\polaris_graph\generator\multi_section_generator.py:2042:11. **Jurisdictional precision (M-29, for multi-authority synthesis)**: When citing regulatory, standards-setting, or governance sources from more than one jurisdiction (different countries, agencies, courts, or rulemaking bodies), attribute every specific assertion to the ONE jurisdiction whose source supports it. Do NOT use generic plural language like "both agencies", "all regulators", "authorities generally", "regulators require", "jurisdictions mandate", or similar when the evidence you cite comes from a single jurisdiction. A boxed warning in one jurisdiction is not the same legal instrument as a precaution in another jurisdiction; a formal contraindication in one framework is not automatically equivalent to a warning in another. If the evidence supports only one jurisdiction's position, write: "Jurisdiction A's framework classifies X as a contraindication [ev_A]. Jurisdiction B's framework addresses X through warnings and precautions [ev_B]." Only collapse to "both" / "all" / "generally" when you have a citation from each referenced jurisdiction in the SAME sentence proving the shared position.

2854:    to inject a Mock or broken classifier without instantiating the

2856:    return OpenRouterScopeAffinityLLM()

2858:
2859:def _classify_scope_with_llm(
2860:    question: str,
2861:    domain: str,
2862:) -> dict | None:
2863:    """Best-effort LLM scope classification, run alongside the
2864:    deterministic template-driven `run_scope_gate`. Returns a
2865:    summary dict or None when disabled / empty input.
2866:
2867:    Per LAW II — failure must NOT gate the sweep. Any exception
2868:    in the LLM path is caught and logged; an UNCERTAIN verdict
2869:    is recorded so operator-review can pick it up.
2870:    """
2871:    if os.environ.get("PG_USE_LLM_SCOPE", "0") == "0":
2872:        return None
2873:    if not question or not isinstance(question, str):
2874:        return None
2875:    try:
2876:        llm = _build_scope_llm()
2877:        config = LLMScopeEligibilityClassifierConfig(
2878:            supported_domains=_SCOPE_LLM_SUPPORTED_DOMAINS,
2879:        )
2880:        classifier = LLMScopeEligibilityClassifier(llm, config)
2881:        classification = classifier.classify(question)
2882:        return {
2883:            "verdict": classification.verdict.value,
2884:            "confidence": classification.confidence,
2885:            "domain": classification.domain,
2886:            "rationale": classification.rationale,
2887:            "template_domain_hint": domain,
2888:        }
2889:    except Exception as exc:
2890:        print(f"[M-INT-4] WARN: scope LLM path failed: {exc}")
2891:        return None
2892:

2950:def _route_query_to_domain(
2951:    classification,
2952:    *,
2953:    requested_domain: str | None = None,
2954:) -> dict | None:
2955:    """Best-effort domain routing for the LLM scope classification.
2956:    Telemetry only — does NOT gate retrieval. PG_USE_DOMAIN_ROUTER=0
2957:    disables. Per LAW II, internal failure returns None (does not raise).
2958:
2959:    Codex round-1 MEDIUM fix (v2): when result.template is None
2960:    (UNKNOWN_DOMAIN, REJECTED_*, MISSING_ADAPTERS), the routing
2961:    summary's `domain` was None — the original LLM-asserted domain
2962:    tag was lost. v2 surfaces the original `requested_domain`
2963:    alongside `domain` so unknown-domain telemetry preserves
2964:    "user asked for X, registry doesn't know it" signal.
2965:    """
2966:    if os.environ.get("PG_USE_DOMAIN_ROUTER", "0") == "0":
2967:        return None
2968:    # Default to classification.domain if caller didn't pass one.
2969:    if requested_domain is None and hasattr(classification, "domain"):
2970:        requested_domain = classification.domain
2971:    try:
2972:        registry = _build_domain_router_registry()
2973:        adapters = _build_domain_router_adapters()
2974:        result = route_to_domain(classification, registry, adapters)
2975:        return {
2976:            "outcome": result.outcome.value,
2977:            "domain": (
2978:                result.template.domain_id if result.template else None
2979:            ),
2980:            "requested_domain": requested_domain,
2981:            "adapter_ids": [a.adapter_id for a in result.adapters],
2982:            "rationale": result.rationale,
2983:        }
2984:    except Exception as exc:
2985:        print(f"[M-INT-5] WARN: domain_router path failed: {exc}")
2986:        return None

1:"""B9 domain-pack loader.
2:
3:Loads the per-domain configuration packs in ``config/domain_packs/`` — each
4:pack owns the domain-specific WEIGHTS and LABELS (report sections, contradiction
5:predicates, qualitative lexicon pointer, source-tier credibility priors, safety
6:policy). A pack is NEVER a hard DROP/CAP; the faithfulness engine remains the
7:only hard gate (CLAUDE.md §-1.3).
8:
9:POLARIS is GENERAL by default: an unknown / blank / unrecognised domain resolves
10:to the ``general`` pack — NEVER ``clinical``. The clinical pack is the only one
11:with ``is_clinical: true``; it is selected only when the clinical domain is
12:positively detected (``domain_signal.is_clinical_domain``).
13:
14:LAW VI: no hard-coded pack content — everything is read from YAML. Fail-loud on a
15:malformed pack (a required key missing), but NEVER fail on an unknown domain
16:(degrade to general).
17:"""

33:_REPO_ROOT = Path(__file__).resolve().parents[3]
34:_DOMAIN_PACKS_DIR = _REPO_ROOT / "config" / "domain_packs"
35:
36:# Codex iter-2 P2.1: map the canonical-8 scope tokens whose pack file uses a
37:# different (more descriptive) name onto the pack name, so e.g. the `tech` scope
38:# token resolves the `technology` pack instead of silently falling back to
39:# general. Free-text classifier labels (economics/policy/science/...) match pack
40:# filenames directly and need no alias.
41:_DOMAIN_PACK_ALIASES: dict[str, str] = {
42:    "tech": "technology",
43:    "workforce": "economics",   # labor/workforce questions are economics-shaped
44:    "due_diligence": "economics",
45:}
46:
47:# Required top-level keys every pack must define (schema contract).
48:_REQUIRED_KEYS = (
49:    "domain",
50:    "is_clinical",
51:    "sections",
52:    "contradiction_predicates",
53:    "qualitative_lexicon",
54:    "source_tier_priors",
55:    "safety_policy",

58:_pack_cache: dict[str, dict[str, Any]] = {}
59:
60:
61:def _packs_dir() -> Path:
62:    """The packs directory, env-overridable for tests (LAW VI)."""
63:    override = os.getenv("PG_DOMAIN_PACKS_DIR")
64:    return Path(override) if override else _DOMAIN_PACKS_DIR
65:
66:
67:def available_packs() -> list[str]:
68:    """Sorted list of domain-pack names present on disk (``<name>.yaml``)."""
69:    d = _packs_dir()
70:    if not d.exists():
71:        return []
72:    return sorted(p.stem for p in d.glob("*.yaml"))
73:
74:
75:def _validate_pack(name: str, data: Any) -> dict[str, Any]:
76:    """Fail-loud schema validation of a single pack dict."""
77:    if not isinstance(data, dict):
78:        raise RuntimeError(
79:            f"domain pack {name!r} did not parse to a mapping "
80:            f"(got {type(data).__name__})."
81:        )
82:    missing = [k for k in _REQUIRED_KEYS if k not in data]
83:    if missing:
84:        raise RuntimeError(
85:            f"domain pack {name!r} missing required keys: {missing}."
86:        )
87:    if not isinstance(data["is_clinical"], bool):
88:        raise RuntimeError(
89:            f"domain pack {name!r}: 'is_clinical' must be a bool."
90:        )
91:    declared = normalize_domain(str(data.get("domain")))
92:    # Codex P2.1: the pack's declared domain MUST match its FILENAME so a
93:    # non-clinical file (e.g. economics.yaml) cannot declare domain: clinical
94:    # and inherit the clinical is_clinical allowance below.
95:    if declared != normalize_domain(name):
96:        raise RuntimeError(
97:            f"domain pack {name!r}: declared domain {declared!r} does not match "
98:            f"its filename. Each pack file must declare its own domain."
99:        )
100:    # Only the clinical pack may declare is_clinical true (single source of the
101:    # clinical specialization — prevents a stray non-clinical pack from
102:    # re-activating clinical logic). Filename-anchored after the check above.
103:    if data["is_clinical"] and declared != CLINICAL_DOMAIN:
104:        raise RuntimeError(
105:            f"domain pack {name!r}: only the clinical pack may set "
106:            f"is_clinical: true (got domain={data.get('domain')!r})."
107:        )
108:    return data
109:
110:
111:def load_domain_pack(domain: Optional[str]) -> dict[str, Any]:
112:    """Load the pack for ``domain``; degrade to the ``general`` pack on unknown.
113:
114:    Never raises on an unknown domain (operator-locked: unknown -> general,
115:    never clinical, never abort). Raises only when the RESOLVED pack file is
116:    malformed (fail-loud) or when neither the requested pack NOR the general
117:    fallback exists on disk (a real config-integrity error).
118:    """
119:    name = normalize_domain(domain)
120:    # Resolve a canonical-8 scope token onto its descriptively-named pack file.
121:    name = _DOMAIN_PACK_ALIASES.get(name, name)
122:    if name in _pack_cache:
123:        return _pack_cache[name]
124:    d = _packs_dir()
125:    path = d / f"{name}.yaml"
126:    if not path.exists():
127:        # Unknown domain -> general fallback (never clinical).
128:        if name != GENERAL_DOMAIN:
129:            return load_domain_pack(GENERAL_DOMAIN)
130:        raise RuntimeError(
131:            f"general domain pack not found at {path}. config/domain_packs/"
132:            f"general.yaml is required (the domain-agnostic default)."
133:        )
134:    raw = yaml.safe_load(path.read_text(encoding="utf-8")) or {}
135:    pack = _validate_pack(name, raw)
136:    _pack_cache[name] = pack
137:    return pack
138:
139:
140:def pack_is_clinical(domain: Optional[str]) -> bool:
141:    """Convenience: does the resolved pack activate clinical rigor?"""
142:    return bool(load_domain_pack(domain).get("is_clinical", False))
143:
144:
145:def _reset_pack_cache() -> None:
146:    """Test hook: clear the loaded-pack cache."""
147:    _pack_cache.clear()

 succeeded in 568ms:
641:# B9 domain-generalization (the spine) — cheap domain/intent classifier.
642:#
643:# SG1 fix: POLARIS historically DEFAULTED to clinical and never INFERRED the
644:# domain, so a non-clinical question silently ran the clinical path. This adds a
645:# lightweight, INJECTABLE domain/intent classifier that returns a free-text
646:# domain (NOT a closed enum — avoids re-introducing the closed-set trap one
647:# level up), the deterministic capability flags `is_clinical` / `is_quantitative`,
648:# and `key_entity_types`. It DEGRADES gracefully: a wrong/blank classification
649:# falls to `general` (weight, never abort, never clinical). The output is
650:# ADVISORY routing context — it is NOT serialized into the immutable
651:# `protocol.json`, so adding it does NOT change the protocol bytes/hash (clinical
652:# byte-identity preserved).
653:#
654:# The LLM seam is OPTIONAL + injectable. The DEFAULT is a deterministic, NO-SPEND
655:# heuristic (reuses the `is_clinical_domain` backbone + the field-agnostic frame
656:# heuristic), so build/smoke never hit a live LLM. A caller may inject an LLM
657:# callable (`Callable[[str], str]` returning JSON) to refine the free-text domain
658:# label; an LLM error/garbage output FAILS OPEN to the heuristic (general).
659:# ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
660:
661:
662:@dataclass(frozen=True)
663:class DomainIntent:
664:    """Advisory domain/intent classification (B9 SG1). NOT serialized into
665:    protocol.json — pure routing context."""
666:
667:    domain: str                  # free-text domain label; "general" when unknown
668:    is_clinical: bool            # the deterministic clinical specialization signal
669:    is_quantitative: bool        # the question asks for a measured quantity
670:    key_entity_types: list[str]  # advisory entity-type hints (free text)
671:    source: str = "heuristic"    # "heuristic" | "llm" — provenance of the label
672:
673:
674:def _heuristic_domain_label(question: str, is_clinical: bool) -> str:
675:    """Deterministic free-text domain label from cheap keyword cues. NO LLM.
676:
677:    Returns a free-text label (NOT a closed enum) — `clinical` when the clinical
678:    signal fired, else the first matching coarse bucket, else `general`. The
679:    label is advisory only; the hard scope-template route uses the SUPPORTED set
680:    with `general` as the safe default, so an unrecognised label never aborts."""
681:    if is_clinical:
682:        return "clinical"
683:    q = (question or "").lower()
684:    # Coarse, additive cue buckets (free text, not a gate). Order = priority.
685:    buckets: list[tuple[str, tuple[str, ...]]] = [
686:        ("economics", ("gdp", "inflation", "unemployment", "labor", "labour",
687:                        "wage", "economic", "economy", "productivity", "market",
688:                        "trade", "fiscal", "monetary", "recession")),
689:        ("policy", ("policy", "regulation", "regulatory", "legislation", "bill ",
690:                    "statute", "governance", "compliance", "subsidy", "tariff")),
691:        ("technology", ("ai ", "artificial intelligence", "machine learning",
692:                        "software", "algorithm", "model ", "semiconductor",
693:                        "compute", "neural", "llm")),
694:        ("science", ("physics", "chemistry", "biology", "climate", "emissions",
695:                     "materials", "genome", "experiment", "quantum")),
696:    ]
697:    for label, cues in buckets:
698:        if any(cue in q for cue in cues):
699:            return label
700:    return GENERAL_DOMAIN
701:
702:
703:def _is_quantitative_question(question: str) -> bool:
704:    """Deterministic: does the question ask for a measured quantity? Reuses the
705:    field-agnostic metric-cue regex (no domain literal)."""
706:    return bool(_FRAME_METRIC_CUES_RE.search(question or ""))
707:
708:
709:def classify_domain_intent(
710:    question: str,
711:    *,
712:    evidence: Optional[list[dict[str, Any]]] = None,
713:    domain_hint: Optional[str] = None,
714:    llm: Optional[Any] = None,
715:) -> DomainIntent:
716:    """Classify the question's domain + intent (B9 SG1). Deterministic by
717:    default; an optional injected `llm` callable (``Callable[[str], str]``
718:    returning a JSON object with a ``domain`` key) only REFINES the free-text
719:    label. Never raises, never aborts, FAILS OPEN to `general`.
720:
721:    Args:
722:        question: the raw research question.
723:        evidence: optional evidence rows; used by the deterministic clinical
724:            signal when `domain_hint` is blank.
725:        domain_hint: optional caller-supplied domain (e.g. the sweep's
726:            `q["domain"]`). An explicit non-blank hint is authoritative for the
727:            clinical signal (so `domain="clinical"` -> is_clinical True).
728:        llm: optional injectable seam (``Callable[[str], str]``). Tests inject a
729:            deterministic fake; production may inject a real Writer call. An LLM
730:            error or unparseable output is swallowed -> heuristic label.
731:
732:    Returns:
733:        DomainIntent. `is_clinical` is the deterministic backbone signal;
734:        `domain` is a free-text label degrading to `general`.
735:    """
736:    from src.polaris_graph.domain.domain_signal import (
737:        GENERAL_DOMAIN as _GENERAL,
738:        is_clinical_domain,
739:        normalize_domain,
740:    )
741:    is_clinical = is_clinical_domain(domain_hint, evidence)
742:    is_quant = _is_quantitative_question(question)
743:    frame = extract_research_frame_heuristic(question)
744:    key_entity_types = frame.get("entities", [])[:6]
745:
746:    # Default deterministic label.
747:    label = _heuristic_domain_label(question, is_clinical)
748:    source = "heuristic"
749:
750:    # Optional LLM refinement (fail-open). The LLM may only OVERRIDE the
751:    # free-text label for a non-clinical question; it NEVER overrides the
752:    # deterministic clinical signal (clinical safety stays deterministic).
753:    if llm is not None and not is_clinical:
754:        try:
755:            raw = llm(question)
756:            parsed = json.loads(raw) if isinstance(raw, str) else (raw or {})
757:            llm_domain = normalize_domain(str(parsed.get("domain", "")))
758:            # Codex P2.2: the LLM may refine a NON-clinical free-text label but
759:            # must NEVER set the label to "clinical" when the deterministic
760:            # is_clinical signal is False — clinical routing stays deterministic
761:            # (a clinical label with is_clinical False would be inconsistent and
762:            # could reactivate clinical routing in a downstream consumer).
763:            if (
764:                llm_domain
765:                and llm_domain != _GENERAL
766:                and llm_domain != "clinical"
767:            ):
768:                label = llm_domain
769:                source = "llm"
770:        except Exception:
771:            # Garbage / error -> keep the deterministic heuristic label.
772:            label = _heuristic_domain_label(question, is_clinical)
773:            source = "heuristic"
774:
775:    return DomainIntent(
776:        domain=label or _GENERAL,
777:        is_clinical=is_clinical,
778:        is_quantitative=is_quant,
779:        key_entity_types=list(key_entity_types),
780:        source=source,
781:    )
782:
783:

785:# Main entry point
786:# ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

788:
789:def run_scope_gate(
790:    *,
791:    research_question: str,
792:    run_dir: Path | str,
793:    run_id: str,
794:    domain: str = DEFAULT_DOMAIN,
795:    user_overrides: Optional[dict[str, Any]] = None,
796:) -> ScopeGateResult:
797:    """Lock the research protocol at T+0 and write protocol.json.
798:
799:    Args:
800:        research_question: The raw user query. Stored verbatim — no
801:            paraphrasing, no summarization.
802:        run_dir: Directory where protocol.json will be written.
803:            Created if it does not exist.
804:        run_id: Stable run identifier (used for audit trail).
805:        domain: clinical / policy / tech / due_diligence. Picks the template.
806:        user_overrides: Optional dict merging into the template. Keys that
807:            exist in the template are replaced verbatim (no deep-merge).
808:            Merge is logged in protocol.user_overrides so every deviation
809:            from the template is traceable.
810:
811:    Returns:
812:        ScopeGateResult with the protocol, file path, and SHA-256 hash.
813:        The protocol file is written atomically (write to .tmp, rename).
814:    """
815:    if not research_question or not research_question.strip():
816:        raise ValueError("research_question must be non-empty.")
817:
818:    # BUG-B-100 fix (deep-dive R3): the scope gate is now an actual gate.
819:    # Track reject/review/proceed explicitly instead of silently coercing
820:    # unsupported inputs. Reject decisions cause the orchestrator to
821:    # abort BEFORE retrieval with manifest.status=abort_scope_rejected.
822:    scope_decision = "proceed"
823:    scope_rejected = False
824:    scope_rejection_code: Optional[str] = None
825:    scope_reasons: list[str] = []
826:
827:    # B9 (Codex P1.1): a BLANK / whitespace-only domain is NOT an "unsupported
828:    # domain" error — it is the domain-LESS case that must route to the
829:    # domain-agnostic general default (operator-locked: "" -> general, never
830:    # abort). Normalize it to DEFAULT_DOMAIN (general) BEFORE the unsupported
831:    # check so it proceeds. A genuinely malformed explicit literal (e.g.
832:    # "made_up_domain") still falls through to the fail-loud reject below.
833:    if not (domain or "").strip():
834:        domain = DEFAULT_DOMAIN
835:
836:    # Hard reject #1: unsupported domain. A blank domain was normalized to
837:    # general above; only a non-blank UNRECOGNISED literal reaches here and
838:    # rejects loudly (fail-loud, never a silent clinical fallback).
839:    if domain not in SUPPORTED_DOMAINS:
840:        scope_decision = "reject"
841:        scope_rejected = True
842:        scope_rejection_code = "unsupported_domain"
843:        scope_reasons.append(
844:            f"domain={domain!r} is not in SUPPORTED_DOMAINS. "
845:            f"Options: {sorted(SUPPORTED_DOMAINS)}."
846:        )
847:        # Keep domain as-is for the record; the orchestrator will
848:        # abort before any domain-specific logic fires.
849:
850:    run_dir_path = Path(run_dir)

 succeeded in 582ms:
77:# B9 domain-generalization — DOMAIN-AGNOSTIC numeric extraction.
78:#
79:# The clinical extractor (`extract_numeric_claims` clinical path) keys a claim
80:# on a CLINICAL predicate lexicon (`_EFFICACY_PREDICATES` / `_SAFETY_PREDICATES`)
81:# and a CLINICAL subject (`_DRUG_NAME_RE`). For a non-clinical corpus
82:# (economics / AI-labor / policy / science / tech) both return nothing, so
83:# `extract_numeric_claims` emits ZERO claims → every non-clinical numeric row
84:# becomes a SINGLETON in `finding_dedup` / `claim_graph` (the documented
85:# residual that blocks B6/B8 non-clinical baskets). The generic path below
86:# extracts a claim-atom WITHOUT any clinical literal: a generic metric cue (the
87:# SAME field-agnostic cues as `scope_gate.extract_research_frame_heuristic`) is
88:# the predicate, a generic number+unit is the value, and a generic content-word
89:# entity is the subject. This yields a REAL claim-key for non-clinical numerics
90:# (so corroborating sources can consolidate into a basket) WITHOUT loosening the
91:# clinical merge rule — clinical runs are byte-identical (gated on is_clinical).
92:# Faithfulness is unchanged: span-grounding / strict_verify remain the hard gate.
93:# ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
94:
95:# Any number with an OPTIONAL trailing unit token. Generic — no clinical
96:# vocabulary. Captures percent / currency-magnitude / plain counts so an
97:# economics or labor numeric ("GDP grew 3.2%", "$13 trillion", "75.5 percent")
98:# is extractable. The unit is normalized so two sources stating the same
99:# quantity in the same unit can consolidate.
100:_GENERIC_VALUE_RE = re.compile(
101:    r"(?P<value>-?\d+(?:,\d{3})*(?:\.\d+)?)\s*"
102:    r"(?P<unit>%|percent|percentage\s*points?|pp|bps|basis\s*points?|"
103:    r"trillion|billion|million|thousand|"
104:    r"usd|eur|gbp|cad|jpy|"
105:    r"gw|mw|kw|kwh|mwh|twh|tonnes?|tons?|"
106:    r"jobs?|workers?|people|users?|points?|x|×)?"
107:    r"(?=\s|[,.;:)\]!?]|$)",
108:    re.IGNORECASE,
109:)
110:
111:
112:def _normalize_generic_unit(unit: str) -> str:
113:    """Canonicalize a generic unit token so equivalent spellings consolidate.
114:
115:    '%'/'percent' -> 'percent'; magnitude words and currencies stay as the
116:    lowercased token. Empty/None -> '' (a unit-less count). Pure."""
117:    u = (unit or "").strip().lower()
118:    if not u:
119:        return ""
120:    u = re.sub(r"\s+", " ", u)
281:def _extract_numeric_claims_generic(
282:    evidence: list[dict[str, Any]],
283:) -> list["ExtractedNumericClaim"]:
284:    """DOMAIN-AGNOSTIC numeric claim extraction for NON-clinical corpora.
285:
286:    One claim per row at most (the first generic metric value), keyed on a
287:    generic predicate + generic subject + value + unit. A row with no generic
288:    metric cue OR no extractable number yields NO claim (kept as a safe
289:    singleton upstream). The clinical discriminator fields (dose / arm / Wave-3)
290:    stay at their UNKNOWN defaults so they never anchor a non-clinical merge —
291:    a non-clinical merge is anchored ONLY by a confidently-extracted
292:    subject+predicate+value+unit (the conservative rule). NO clinical literal.
293:    """
294:    claims: list[ExtractedNumericClaim] = []
295:    for ev in evidence:
296:        quote = ev.get("direct_quote") or ev.get("statement") or ""
297:        if not quote:
298:            continue
299:        predicate = _normalize_predicate_generic(quote)
300:        if not predicate:
301:            continue
302:        # Anchor the value on the number nearest the metric cue (Codex P1.4) so
303:        # a leading date / sample-size cannot become the claim value/key.
304:        _cue_m = re.search(re.escape(predicate), quote, flags=re.IGNORECASE)
305:        _cue_pos = _cue_m.start() if _cue_m else None
306:        found = _find_value_generic(quote, cue_pos=_cue_pos)
307:        if found is None:
308:            continue
309:        value, unit, ctx_window, anchor_pos = found
310:        subject = _subject_generic(quote, anchor_pos, predicate=predicate)
311:        # Codex iter-3 P1: do NOT set endpoint_phrase=predicate. The metric cue
312:        # IS the predicate, not a time-window/endpoint. Stamping it as
313:        # endpoint_phrase would make _shared_metric_axes read two same-predicate
314:        # generic claims as POSITIVELY-confirmed shared scope -> a hard
315:        # contradiction, when in fact their comparator/population/time-window are
316:        # UNCONFIRMED. Leaving the discriminator axes empty means an unconfirmed
317:        # generic numeric gap is correctly labeled possible_metric_mismatch
318:        # (conservative non-clinical default); a true contradiction needs a
319:        # positively-extracted shared scope axis. The predicate itself still
320:        # groups the claims (grouping key includes predicate).
321:        claims.append(ExtractedNumericClaim(
322:            evidence_id=str(ev.get("evidence_id", "")),
323:            subject=subject,
324:            predicate=predicate,
325:            value=value,
326:            unit=unit,
327:            context_snippet=ctx_window[:200],
328:            source_url=ev.get("source_url", ""),
329:            source_tier=ev.get("tier", ""),
330:        ))
331:    return claims

334:# Predicate keywords — the ones we most want to catch contradictions on.
335:# BUG-M-202 fix (deep-dive R7): expanded coverage beyond the original
336:# obesity/cardiometabolic-only set. A proper domain-YAML-driven
337:# profile loader is tracked as a follow-up (see docs/todo_list.md);
338:# this expansion is the minimum-viable fix to close the AF
339:# anticoagulation silent-failure reproducer.
340:_EFFICACY_PREDICATES_METABOLIC = (
341:    # Original obesity / GLP-1 / lipid
342:    "weight loss", "body weight", "weight reduction",
343:    "hba1c reduction", "a1c reduction",
344:    "systolic blood pressure reduction", "diastolic blood pressure reduction",
345:    "ldl reduction", "cholesterol reduction",
1060:def extract_numeric_claims(
1061:    evidence: list[dict[str, Any]],
1062:    domain: str | None = None,
1063:) -> list[ExtractedNumericClaim]:
1064:    """Extract structured numeric claims from evidence rows.
1065:
1066:    Each evidence dict should have 'evidence_id', 'direct_quote' (or
1067:    'statement'), 'source_url', 'tier'. Missing fields are handled.
1068:
1069:    BUG-M-202 fix (deep-dive R7): `domain` parameter routes to a
1070:    broader predicate set for non-clinical queries. Default None
1071:    preserves the union-fallback (original behavior).
1072:
1073:    B9 domain-generalization: for a NON-clinical run (deterministic
1074:    `is_clinical_domain` over `domain` + the evidence text) the clinical
1075:    predicate union + `_DRUG_NAME_RE` subject extract NOTHING, so this
1076:    historically returned zero claims and every non-clinical numeric became a
1077:    singleton. We now route non-clinical corpora to the DOMAIN-AGNOSTIC
1078:    extractor (generic metric cue + generic subject + value + unit), which
1079:    yields a real claim-key so corroborating sources can consolidate. The
1080:    CLINICAL path (is_clinical True — `domain="clinical"`, or a blank domain
1081:    over a clinically-signalled corpus) is byte-identical to before.
1082:    """
1083:    from src.polaris_graph.domain.domain_signal import is_clinical_domain
1084:    if not is_clinical_domain(domain, evidence):
1085:        return _extract_numeric_claims_generic(evidence)
1086:    claims: list[ExtractedNumericClaim] = []
1087:    for ev in evidence:
1088:        quote = ev.get("direct_quote") or ev.get("statement") or ""
1089:        if not quote:
1090:            continue
1220:    claims: list[ExtractedNumericClaim],
1221:    *,
1222:    rel_threshold: Optional[float] = None,
1223:    abs_threshold: Optional[float] = None,
1224:    is_clinical: bool = True,
1225:) -> list[ContradictionRecord]:
1226:    """Group claims by (subject, predicate, unit, dose) and flag discrepancies.
1227:
1228:    Fix-1: grouping key now includes DOSE. A 2.4 mg weight-loss result
1229:    and a 7.2 mg weight-loss result are NOT a contradiction — they're
1230:    expected dose-response differences. They will only be grouped
1231:    together if both happen to have no dose tag (e.g., a narrative
1232:    review that doesn't specify dose).
1233:
1234:    B9 domain-generalization (`is_clinical`, default True = byte-identical):
1235:    on a NON-clinical run a generic numeric discrepancy is only a TRUE
1236:    contradiction when the two numbers measure the SAME thing — same
1237:    comparator, population, and endpoint/time-window. When those discriminators
1238:    differ (or cannot be confirmed shared), the pair is labeled
1239:    `possible_metric_mismatch` (severity downgraded, never silently dropped)
1240:    rather than asserted as a hard contradiction. Clinical runs keep the full
1241:    clinical contradiction rule unchanged (this branch never fires on them).
1242:    """
1243:    if rel_threshold is None:
1244:        rel_threshold = PG_CONTRADICTION_REL_THRESHOLD
1245:    if abs_threshold is None:
1246:        abs_threshold = PG_CONTRADICTION_ABS_THRESHOLD
1247:
1248:    grouped: dict[tuple[str, str, str, str], list[ExtractedNumericClaim]] = {}
1249:    for c in claims:
1250:        key = (c.subject, c.predicate, c.unit, c.dose or "")
1251:        grouped.setdefault(key, []).append(c)
1252:
1253:    records: list[ContradictionRecord] = []
1254:    for (subject, predicate, unit, dose), group in grouped.items():
1255:        if len(group) < 2:
1256:            continue
1257:        values = [c.value for c in group]
1258:        vmin, vmax = min(values), max(values)
1259:        denom = max(abs(vmin), 1e-9)
1260:        rel = abs(vmax - vmin) / denom
1261:        abs_diff = abs(vmax - vmin)
1262:        if rel >= rel_threshold and abs_diff >= abs_threshold:
1263:            predicate_display = predicate
1264:            if dose:
1265:                predicate_display = f"{predicate} ({dose})"
1266:            # B9: on a non-clinical run, a numeric gap is a TRUE contradiction
1267:            # only when the claims share comparator/population/time-window. If
1268:            # those discriminators differ or cannot be confirmed, label it a
1269:            # `possible_metric_mismatch` (downgraded, surfaced, never dropped).
1270:            metric_mismatch = (
1271:                not is_clinical and not _shared_metric_axes(group)
1272:            )
1273:            if metric_mismatch:
1274:                predicate_display = f"{predicate_display} [possible_metric_mismatch]"
1275:                severity = "low"
1276:                action = (
1277:                    "Possible metric mismatch (B9): these numbers may not measure "
1278:                    "the same quantity — comparator, population, or time-window "
1279:                    "could differ. Disclose both with their scope; do NOT assert a "
1280:                    "contradiction without a confirmed shared metric."
1281:                )
1282:                records.append(ContradictionRecord(
1283:                    subject=subject,
1284:                    predicate=predicate_display,
1285:                    claims=sorted(group, key=lambda c: c.value),

1:"""B9 domain-generalization spine — the deterministic ``is_clinical`` backbone.
2:
3:POLARIS is GENERAL by default; clinical rigor is a DETECTED specialization
4:layered on top (dual-agreed B1-B10 plan, B9 "the spine"). The historical
5:failure (the 2,738 junk clinical-contradiction flags on a labor-displacement
6:report) was NOT "a domain param wasn't read" alone — it was that the clinical
7:predicate/lexicon logic fired UNCONDITIONALLY on every domain. The fix is a
8:single, deterministic, NO-LLM ``is_clinical`` signal threaded into every
9:consumer (qualitative_conflict_detector, contradiction_detector, finding_dedup,
10:multi_section_generator). When ``is_clinical`` is False the clinical-only
11:predicate unions / NegEx lexicon / clinical few-shots do not apply; the
12:domain-agnostic path runs instead. The faithfulness engine (strict_verify /
13:span-grounding / NLI / 4-role) is unchanged and remains the only hard gate.
14:
15:Design rules (operator-locked B9 constraints):
16:
17:1. **DETERMINISTIC, NO LLM.** This is the backbone every consumer reads; it
18:   must be pure, offline, and cheap. The LLM domain/intent classifier in
19:   ``scope_gate`` is a SECONDARY augmentation that degrades to this signal.
20:2. **Clinical only on a POSITIVE signal.** ``is_clinical`` is True iff the
21:   domain is the explicit ``"clinical"`` token OR (when the domain is
22:   blank/unknown) a clinical intervention OR a clinical population marker is
23:   positively present in the supplied text. Any non-clinical KNOWN domain
24:   (workforce / policy / tech / economics / general / custom / ...) is
25:   ``False`` regardless of text — a labor report that happens to quote the
26:   word "mortality" must NOT route clinical.
27:3. **Byte-identity for clinical.** A clinical run today passes
28:   ``domain="clinical"`` (the sweep reads ``q["domain"]``) → this returns True →
29:   the clinical path is taken unchanged. The ``domain=None`` default (tests +
30:   legacy callers) falls back to the text-signal probe so a genuinely clinical
31:   corpus with no domain hint still routes clinical (no regression).
32:4. **Never abort.** This module only classifies; it never raises on input and
33:   never holds a report. Unknown → general.
34:
35:LAW VI: the clinical recognizers reused here are config-driven (the
36:``scope_gate`` intervention recognizer reads
37:``config/clinical_safety/intervention_recognition.yaml``).
38:"""
39:
40:from __future__ import annotations
41:
42:from typing import Any, Iterable, Optional
43:
44:# The canonical clinical-domain token. The sweep supplies a per-query
45:# ``domain`` string; "clinical" is the ONE value that forces the clinical
46:# specialization. Everything else (incl. "" / None / "general" / "workforce" /
47:# "policy" / "tech" / "economics" / "custom") is non-clinical.
48:CLINICAL_DOMAIN = "clinical"
49:
50:# The general default. A blank / unknown / unrecognised domain routes here —
51:# NEVER to clinical (operator-locked: "" -> general).
52:GENERAL_DOMAIN = "general"
53:
54:
55:def normalize_domain(domain: Optional[str]) -> str:
56:    """Lowercase + trim a domain string; blank/None -> ``GENERAL_DOMAIN``.
57:
58:    Pure. Never raises. The single place "" -> general is enforced so no
59:    consumer accidentally falls back to clinical.
60:    """
61:    d = (domain or "").strip().lower()
62:    return d if d else GENERAL_DOMAIN
63:
64:
65:def _row_texts(evidence: Optional[Iterable[Any]]) -> list[str]:
66:    """Best-effort text fields from evidence rows (direct_quote/statement/text/
67:    title). Tolerates dicts and missing keys; never raises."""
68:    texts: list[str] = []
69:    for ev in evidence or []:
70:        if not isinstance(ev, dict):
71:            continue
72:        for key in ("direct_quote", "statement", "text", "title"):
73:            val = ev.get(key)
74:            if val:
75:                texts.append(str(val))
76:    return texts
77:
78:
79:# Clinical endpoint / safety vocabulary — a clinical SIGNAL even when no drug is
80:# named in the same span (e.g. "Achieved 14.9% weight loss from baseline.").
81:# These are clinical-SPECIFIC; a non-clinical economics / labor / policy corpus
82:# does not use them. Kept here (not imported from contradiction_detector) to
83:# avoid an import cycle. Lowercase substring match.
84:_CLINICAL_ENDPOINT_TERMS: tuple[str, ...] = (
85:    "weight loss", "hba1c", "a1c", "blood pressure", "ldl", "cholesterol",
86:    "mace", "all-cause mortality", "cardiovascular", "adverse event",
87:    "serious adverse event", "contraindicated", "contraindication",
88:    "drug interaction", "hypoglycemia", "pancreatitis",
89:    "discontinuation rate", "placebo-controlled", "randomized controlled",
90:    "double-blind", "phase 3 trial", "phase iii trial", "efficacy endpoint",
91:    "primary endpoint", "comorbidit",
92:)
93:
94:# Clinical-SPECIFIC population markers (NOT generic "adults"/"children"/
95:# "elderly", which appear in non-clinical labor/policy corpora too — Codex P1.1).
96:# A disease/condition population is a positive clinical signal; a bare age band
97:# is not. Lowercase substring match.
98:_CLINICAL_POPULATION_TERMS: tuple[str, ...] = (
99:    "type 1 diabetes", "type 2 diabetes", "t1dm", "t2dm", "diabetic",
100:    "obesity", "overweight", "obese",
101:    "chronic kidney disease", "ckd", "renal impairment",
102:    "heart failure", "nafld", "nash", "mash", "masld",
103:    "pregnant", "postmenopausal", "premenopausal",
104:    "patients with", "in patients", "the patient",
105:)
106:
107:
108:def _has_clinical_text_signal(evidence: Optional[Iterable[Any]]) -> bool:
109:    """True iff a clinical INTERVENTION, clinical-SPECIFIC condition population,
110:    or clinical ENDPOINT/safety marker is positively present in the evidence
111:    text.
112:
113:    Codex P1.1: a GENERIC age-band population ("adults"/"children"/"elderly")
114:    does NOT count — those appear in non-clinical labor/policy corpora too. A
115:    positive clinical signal requires a drug, a disease/condition population, or
116:    clinical endpoint vocabulary. Reuses the ``scope_gate`` config-driven
117:    intervention recognizer (INN stems + known-name seed list, LAW VI). Import
118:    is local to avoid a heavy import cycle; recognizer/config errors are
119:    swallowed (fail-soft, never clinical-by-error).
120:    """
121:    texts = _row_texts(evidence)
122:    if not texts:
123:        return False
124:    blob_l = "\n".join(texts).lower()
125:    # Clinical endpoint / safety vocabulary (cheap substring; no config).
126:    for term in _CLINICAL_ENDPOINT_TERMS:
127:        if term in blob_l:
128:            return True
129:    # Clinical-SPECIFIC condition population (NOT a bare age band).
130:    for term in _CLINICAL_POPULATION_TERMS:
131:        if term in blob_l:
132:            return True
133:    # Intervention (config-driven drug recognizer). Probe per-row so an early
134:    # hit short-circuits; recognizer/config errors are non-fatal.
135:    try:
136:        from src.polaris_graph.nodes.scope_gate import _intervention_present
137:        for text in texts:
138:            if _intervention_present(text):
139:                return True
140:    except Exception:
141:        return False
142:    return False
143:
144:
145:def is_clinical_domain(
146:    domain: Optional[str],
147:    evidence: Optional[Iterable[Any]] = None,
148:) -> bool:
149:    """Return True iff the run should take the CLINICAL specialization.
150:
151:    Deterministic, no LLM, never raises. The single ``is_clinical`` signal
152:    every B9 consumer reads.
153:
154:    Routing (in order):
155:      * ``domain == "clinical"`` (case/space-insensitive) -> True. This is the
156:        explicit positive route the sweep takes for clinical questions, so the
157:        clinical path is byte-identical to today.
158:      * Any OTHER known/non-blank domain (workforce/policy/tech/economics/
159:        general/custom/...) -> False. A non-clinical domain NEVER routes
160:        clinical even if the text mentions a clinical-sounding word.
161:      * Blank / None domain -> probe the evidence text for a positive clinical
162:        intervention or population signal; True only on a positive signal,
163:        else False. This keeps a genuinely-clinical corpus with no domain hint
164:        (tests / legacy callers that pass ``domain=None``) on the clinical
165:        path with no regression, while a blank non-clinical corpus stays
166:        general.
167:    """
168:    d = (domain or "").strip().lower()
169:    if d == CLINICAL_DOMAIN:
170:        return True
171:    if d:
172:        # A positively-named non-clinical domain is authoritative.
173:        return False
174:    # Blank/unknown domain: degrade to a positive text signal, never to
175:    # clinical-by-default.
176:    return _has_clinical_text_signal(evidence)

602:def _relevance_drop_ledger_enabled() -> bool:
603:    """Kill-switch `PG_RELEVANCE_DROP_LEDGER` (default ON under semantic_v2). When
604:    ON, every below-floor relevance drop is logged with its cosine score + url so
605:    the operator can audit the filter. Telemetry-only — never changes the keep
606:    set. Default-ON is harmless when the semantic scorer is OFF (no drops are made
607:    by the semantic filter on the legacy path)."""
608:    raw = os.environ.get("PG_RELEVANCE_DROP_LEDGER", "1").strip().lower()

612:# Module-level cache: `prefetch_offtopic_filter._load_embedder()` constructs a
613:# fresh `EmbeddingService` per call (it does NOT use the singleton), so cache the
614:# handle here to reuse the already-resident MiniLM weights across rows / sections.
615:# Sentinel `False` = "tried and failed to load" (distinct from `None` = "not yet
616:# tried") so the loud-fallback path fires exactly once, not on every section.
617:_SEMANTIC_EMBEDDER_CACHE: Any = None
618:
619:
620:def _get_semantic_embedder() -> Any:
621:    """Return the cached embedder (reusing `prefetch_offtopic_filter`'s loader so
622:    we share the same MiniLM weights / model cost). Returns the embedder, or None
623:    if it could not be loaded — the caller MUST handle None with a LOUD fallback
624:    to the lexical scorer (LAW II: no silent degrade)."""
625:    global _SEMANTIC_EMBEDDER_CACHE
626:    if _SEMANTIC_EMBEDDER_CACHE is None:
627:        try:
628:            from src.polaris_graph.retrieval.prefetch_offtopic_filter import (
629:                _load_embedder,
630:            )
631:            loaded = _load_embedder()
632:        except Exception as exc:  # import-path failure
633:            _LOGGER.warning(
634:                "[select] semantic relevance: embedder import failed (%s) — "
635:                "the caller will fall back LOUDLY to the lexical scorer.",
636:                str(exc)[:200],
637:            )
638:            loaded = None
639:        # False sentinel = "load attempted, unavailable" so we don't retry per row.
640:        _SEMANTIC_EMBEDDER_CACHE = loaded if loaded is not None else False
641:    return _SEMANTIC_EMBEDDER_CACHE if _SEMANTIC_EMBEDDER_CACHE else None
