high internal consistency (Cronbach's alpha = 0.79–0.93 across parts) and strong correlation with the original UPDRS (ρ = 0.96), providing a comprehensive framework for tracking these changes across changes from baseline to six months in the quality of life, as assessed by the Parkinson's Disease Questionnaire (PDQ-39), and the severity of symptoms without medication, according to the Unified Parkinson's Disease Rating Scale, part III (UPDRS-III).[1] Timepoint: six months.[1] Study design: randomized-pairs trial.[1]\n\n### Mechanism\n\nComplication type: hardware complications.[2] The Movement Disorder Society-sponsored Unified Parkinson's Disease Rating Scale, known as the MDS-UPDRS, structures the clinical assessment of Parkinson's disease into four distinct and complementary parts.[3] Whereas Part I addresses the non-motor domain, Part II shifts the focus to motor aspects of daily function.[3] However, Part IV specifically records motor complications, distinguishing them from the core motor examination.[3] The combined clinimetric results of this study support the validity of the MDS-UPDRS for rating PD.[3] The primary source identifies hardware complications as a distinct category of DBS-related adverse events.[2] In contrast, the source does not offer guidance on daily life adjustments or support strategies for patients experiencing hardware complications.[2]\n\n### DBS device contraindications and dopaminergic-withdrawal dosing safety\n\nContract-bound content for dbs_device_mri_safety did not survive strict verification against retrieved primary source text; this slot is a curator-actionable gap. Rating Scale (MDS-UPDRS), validated in a study of 877 patients from 39 sites, demonstrated high internal consistency (Cronbach's alpha = 0.79–0.93 across parts) and strong correlation with the original UPDRS (ρ = 0.96), providing a comprehensive framework for tracking these changes across four parts: non-motor experiences of daily living, motor experiences of daily living, motor examination, and motor complications.[3] In advanced stages, symptoms are severe and daily life usually requires full-time support.[5]\n\n### Safety\n\nThe FDA labeling for carbidopa-levodopa explicitly warns that patients should be observed carefully when the dosage is reduced abruptly or discontinued, especially if they are receiving neuroleptics.[6] DBS hardware complications are relatively common; in a prospective series of 81 consecutive patients with PD undergoing subthalamic nucleus (STN) DBS followed for a mean of 17 months, hardware complications occurred in 26.2% of patients (atom_009).[2] Specific complications included infections requiring system removal in 2.5% (atom_010) and implantable pulse generator (IPG) removal in 3.7% (atom_011), misplaced leads in 12.5% (atom_012), and lead migration, fracture, or IPG malfunction.[2] A review of the FDA MAUDE database over 10 years identified 221 unique adverse events, with infection being the most common (16.2%) (atom_013) and lead migration accounting for 8.6% (atom_014).[7] Over a third (40%) of all devices with adverse events required return to the operating room for explant or revision (atom_003, atom_004).[7] A review of postoperative psychosis after DBS reported a lifetime incidence of psychosis of 28.3% (atom_018) in PD patients following DBS, underscoring the need for vigilance.[8] Additionally, implantable pulse generator expiration can precipitate a movement disorder emergency with severe parkinsonian worsening or akinetic crisis, necessitating urgent medical evaluation.[9] Abrupt reduction or discontinuation of carbidopa-levodopa has been associated with a symptom complex resembling neuroleptic malignant syndrome (NMS), characterized by hyperthermia, muscle rigidity, altered consciousness, and autonomic dysfunction.[6] Psychosis can emerge after STN DBS, as illustrated by clinical cases where patients developed paranoid ideation, hallucinations, and delusions within months of surgery, requiring antipsychotic treatment.[8] Patients taking dopaminergic medications may experience intense urges to gamble, increased sexual urges, binge eating, and other compulsive behaviors, which may resolve with dose reduction or discontinuation[6]\n\n### Population Subgroups\n\nThe choice between rechargeable and non‑rechargeable IPGs was significantly associated with affordability (χ²=19.13, p<0.001), and remote programming capability influenced brand selection.[10] Despite this, a preliminary survey of 17 DBS patients (85% of those approached) found that 76.5% used their patient programmer once per month or less, 17.7% never used it, and the most common function was checking battery life (58.8%).[11] Adaptive DBS shows promise, as one study reported that overall well‑being significantly improved with adaptive stimulation (p=0.007).[12] Caregivers of intermediate PD spent 6.6 hours per day caring, compared to 3.2 hours for early PD, and were less likely to be employed (25.3% vs 42.4%).[13] Personalized DBS programming using machine learning to optimize gait has produced meaningful improvements in walking, addressing a common post‑DBS challenge.[14] Remote patient monitoring devices led 79.9% of participants to agree that they improved mobility, and 48% perceived themselves as safer.[15] However, a meta‑synthesis of wearable device experiences identified barriers including discomfort, emotional distress, and interface difficulties that hinder long‑term adherence.[16] 87.3% of DBS patients used rechargeable IPGs (r-IPGs), 87.7% were satisfied with their device; among those with r-IPGs, 40.6% felt confident handling the device within one week of discharge, 71.4% recharged independently, and the mean recharge interval was 4.3 days.[10] Caregivers of people with advanced PD had significantly higher perceived burden (ZBI 34.5 vs 24.7 for early PD) and were more likely to take medication due to caregiving (34.7% vs 13.8%).[13] Assistive technologies also contribute: a scoping review reported that home automation significantly increased quality of life (p<0.001), and 63.5% of participants used speech‑to‑text functions to cope with tremors.[15]\n\n### Limitations\n\nLimitations: The pipeline's tier distribution reveals a heavy reliance on lower-quality evidence, with only 10% of sources classified as T1 primary studies and a striking 70% falling into the UNKNOWN tier. Six high-severity contradictions were detected, including a 972.4% relative difference in mortality estimates and a 481.0% divergence in hazard ratio, indicating sources disagree sharply on both magnitude and direction of key outcomes. The evidence horizon spans from 2010-01-01 to the present, but the predominance of unclassified sources and sparse representation in tiers T2 (1%), T3 (1%), and T5 (0%) further limit confidence in the synthesized findings.\n\n## Methods\nPre-registered protocol.json (SHA-256 36a59dc009aa949f...).\nCorpus: Serper + Semantic Scholar + OpenAlex live retrieval, augmented by domain backends (clinical: domain_backends(clinical): {'europe_pmc': 27}).\nRetrieval fetch outcome: 478 of 740 candidate sources fetched; 262 failed or timed out.\nGenerator model: deepseek/deepseek-v4-pro (multi-section: outline + 6 parallel sections + strict_verify + regen-on-failure).\nEvaluator model: google/gemma-4-31b-it (different family).\nSources classified using T1-T7 tier taxonomy.\nInclusion / exclusion per clinical template. tests\fixtures\redaction_fixture_redact001.json:1160:      "report_text": "## Key Findings\n\n_Each finding below is a verbatim, span-verified statement carried up from the body section named in bold; citations are the body's._\n\n- **Technical_Standard.** ### SAE J3016 driving-automation levels and the human-machine boundary\n\nLevel 5:\nFull Driving Automation.[1]\n- **Legal_Framework.** 157 - Automated Lane Keeping Systems (ALKS).[2]\n- **Background.** The Society of Automotive Engineers (SAE) International standard J3016_202104 defines six levels of driving automation, ranging from Level 0 (no driving automation) to Level 5 (full driving automation), and establishes a taxonomy for on-road motor vehicle driving automation systems.[1]\n- **Key Findings.** In the European Union, a 2025 European Parliament study advocates for a strict liability regime targeting high-risk AI systems, structured around a single responsible operator to avoid regulatory fragmentation among Member States.[15]\n- **Evidence and Analysis.** The EU’s revised Product Liability Directive explicitly extends to software and AI-driven products, including autonomous vehicles, and introduces presumptions of defect and causation to ease the burden on injured parties.[15][24]\n- **Comparative Assessment.** A key challenge across jurisdictions is allocating liability when semi-autonomous systems require constant human oversight, as drivers may over-rely on automation, leading to mode confusion and delayed re-engagement.[17][9]\n\n### SAE J3016 driving-automation levels and the human-machine boundary\n\nLevel 5:\nFull Driving Automation.[1] Standard id: J3016_202104.[1] The SAE J3016_202104 standard defines six levels of driving automation.[1] Level 0 is No Driving Automation.[1] In contrast, Level 1 is Driver Assistance.[1] Level 0 and Level 1 are No Driving Automation and Driver Assistance, respectively.[1] Finally, Level 5 is Full Driving Automation.[1] These are No Driving Automation, Driver Assistance, Partial Driving Automation, Conditional Driving Automation, High Driving Automation, and Full Driving Automation, respectively.[1] Although Level 0 is No Driving Automation, Level 1 is Driver Assistance.[1] In contrast, Level 2 is Partial Driving Automation, whereas Level 3 is Conditional Driving Automation.[1] The taxonomy's six levels are No Driving Automation, Driver Assistance, Partial Driving Automation, Conditional Driving Automation, High Driving Automation, and Full Driving Automation.[1]\n\n### UNECE WP.29 ALKS regulation (system-vs-driver responsibility)\n\nInstrument: UN Regulation No. 157 - Automated Lane Keeping Systems (ALKS).[2] UN Regulation No. 157 - Automated Lane Keeping Systems (ALKS) is the instrument identified in the primary source.[2] The subject matter is Automated Lane Keeping Systems.[2] In contrast, the regulation’s title clearly indicates its focus on automated lane keeping.[2] The regulation’s full designation is UN Regulation No. 157 - Automated Lane Keeping Systems (ALKS).[2] The regulation, UN Regulation No. 157, addresses Automated Lane Keeping Systems.[2]\n\n### NHTSA automated-driving-systems policy and crash-reporting order\n\nScope: Entities named in the General Order.[3] Issuing agency: NHTSA.[3] The scope of this order is precisely defined, applying exclusively to entities named in the General Order.[3] However, the reporting obligation is not triggered for every crash involving those entities; it is conditional upon specific temporal and outcome-based criteria.[3] Alternatively, the obligation arises if the crash involved a vulnerable road user being struck.[3] In contrast, a crash that does not meet these consequence thresholds, even if ADS was active within the 30-second window, would not require reporting under the order.[3] The reporting criteria also encompass an air bag deployment.[3] Finally, any individual being transported to a hospital for medical treatment as a result of the crash mandates a report.[3] By comparison, crashes where ADS was not in use within 30 seconds, or where the damage is below the specified property damage threshold, fall outside the mandatory reporting scope.[3]\n\n### Product-liability / negligence doctrine for ADAS manufacturers\n\nDefect categories: manufacturing defects; design defects; and inadequate instructions or warnings defects.[4]\n\n### Relevant ADAS / automated-feature crash-liability case law\n\nContract-bound content for adas_crash_case_law did not survive strict verification against retrieved primary source text; this slot is a curator-actionable gap. See manifest.frame_coverage_report and human_gap_tasks.json for per-entity detail.[5]\n\n### Proposed regulatory guidelines and liability-allocation recommendations\n\nContract-bound content for proposed_regulatory_guidelines did not survive strict verification against retrieved primary source text; this slot is a curator-actionable gap. See manifest.frame_coverage_report and human_gap_tasks.json for per-entity detail.[6]\n\n### Background\n\nThe Society of Automotive Engineers (SAE) International standard J3016_202104 defines six levels of driving automation, ranging from Level 0 (no driving automation) to Level 5 (full driving automation), and establishes a taxonomy for on-road motor vehicle driving automation systems.[1] Active safety systems such as electronic stability control (ESC) and automatic emergency braking (AEB) are excluded from this taxonomy because they provide only momentary intervention rather than sustained performance of the dynamic driving task (DDT).[1] Common ADAS functions include adaptive cruise control (ACC), blind spot detection (BSD), lane departure warning/lane keeping systems (LDW/LKS), intelligent headlamp control (IHC), and emergency brake assist (EBA).[7] Large-scale crash statistics indicate that among 7.7 million vehicles manufactured between 2015 and 2023, ADAS-equipped cars experienced on average a 27% reduction in forward-collision crashes and an 11% reduction in lane-departure crashes.[8] Blind Spot Monitoring (BSM) systems are estimated to reduce lane-change crashes by 14% and lane-change crashes with injuries by 23%.[9] For example, lane departure warning (LDW) systems were found to be turned off in 67.2% of observed vehicles due to lack of trust and excessive false alarms.[10] Camera-based DMS have been shown to detect driver distraction approximately 50 seconds sooner than steering-wheel-based DMS, and users of steering-wheel DMS were able to engage in 5.65 minutes of continuous distracted driving before an alert, compared to 2.25 minutes for camera-based DMS.[11] The increasing complexity of ADAS and automated driving systems has prompted the development of specialized accident investigation frameworks, such as a comprehensive system proposed in China that integrates traffic accident boundary analysis, control action safety analysis, and causal scene restoration.[12] To evaluate ADAS safety effectiveness, researchers have identified 94 influence parameters from sources including published literature, standardized tests, accident databases, and autonomous vehicle disengagement reports, with 77 parameters assigned importance levels through cluster analysis.[13] Driver drowsiness remains a significant safety concern, with the World Health Organization reporting approximately 1.35 million traffic-related deaths annually, and research indicating that steering wheel angle variability increases from 0.5–1.5° in alert drivers to 2–5° in fatigued states.[14] If the driver does not fully understand the capabilities and limitations of the system, this might lead to mode confusion and can cause surprises for the driver.[9] In the European Union, several SAE Level 0 ADAS will be mandatory by 2022, while no automated driving technology is yet permitted on the EU market.[9]\n\n### Key Findings\n\nIn the European Union, a 2025 European Parliament study advocates for a strict liability regime targeting high-risk AI systems, structured around a single responsible operator to avoid regulatory fragmentation among Member States.[15] This verdict signals a shift toward manufacturer accountability for AI driving systems.[16] NHTSA investigations have identified patterns of failures in Tesla's ADAS, including a probe of 2.4 million vehicles for crashes at intersections and emergency vehicle collisions, and a subsequent probe of 2.88 million vehicles involving 58 traffic violation reports, 14 crashes, and 23 injuries.[16] One analysis argues that semi-autonomous systems create a collaborative driving endeavor, and that automakers' marketing efforts to gain public trust may render them primarily liable when that trust is misplaced, with a reduction for the driver's comparative fault.[17] The opacity of AI decision-making and the 'problem of many hands'—diffusion of responsibility among multiple actors—create a liability gap that traditional tort frameworks struggle to address.[18] A growing body of policy analysis advocates for strict liability for high-risk AI systems, with proposals ranging from a single responsible operator under EU law to no-fault insurance schemes.[15].[18][19] One scholar proposes extending strict products liability to defective software components in autonomous vehicles, treating software creators as strictly liable for injuries caused by dangerously defective code.[20] A law and economics analysis suggests that negligence-based rules can efficiently accommodate AV-human interactions, but require substantial doctrinal reforms, including reconceptualization of fault standards for both AVs and human drivers.[21] Jurisdictional approaches vary: Michigan's product liability statutes govern claims involving defective ADAS, Louisiana applies a comparative fault system to apportion liability between driver and manufacturer, and the UK's Automated and Electric Vehicles Act 2018 explicitly addresses liability for accidents resulting from unauthorized software alterations or failure to update software.[22][23][16]\n\n### Evidence and Analysis\n\nThe EU’s revised Product Liability Directive explicitly extends to software and AI-driven products, including autonomous vehicles, and introduces presumptions of defect and causation to ease the burden on injured parties.[15][24] In China, the undefined legal status of autonomous driving systems has prompted the development of a comprehensive accident investigation system that integrates boundary analysis, control action safety assessment, and causal scene restoration to allocate responsibility.[12] US legal scholarship argues that automakers’ marketing of ADAS features fosters driver trust, and when that trust is misplaced, products liability law supports primary manufacturer liability with a potential reduction for the driver’s comparative fault, rather than placing full responsibility on the human operator.[17] The revised PLD is an EU instrument for AI-related harm[15][24]\n\n### Comparative Assessment\n\nA key challenge across jurisdictions is allocating liability when semi-autonomous systems require constant human oversight, as drivers may over-rely on automation, leading to mode confusion and delayed re-engagement.[17][9] Under U.S. products liability law, automakers may face primary liability for ADAS failures, though the driver's comparative fault can reduce recovery[17][22] Legal scholars debate whether the human driver or the manufacturer bears responsibility when supervised autonomy fails in semi-autonomous vehicles.[17]\n\n### Implications\n\nIn the United Kingdom, the Automated and Electric Vehicles Act 2018 addresses liability for accidents caused by automated vehicles, including those resulting from unauthorized software alterations or failure to update software.[16] Texas has legislatively limited lessors’ liability for failing to retrofit optional safety systems, provided the vehicle met federal standards at purchase.[25] Empirical evidence from a survey of 260 drivers indicates that collision avoidance performance and system reliability are the strongest predictors of user trust, while road safety impact emerges as an independent societal benefit.[26] On the cost side, an Italian analysis estimates that an average ADAS investment of €1,629 per vehicle yields nationwide savings of €1.89 billion in 2024, with most systems becoming cost-effective within two to three years.[27] Daimler Trucks North America was granted summary judgment in a lawsuit.[25] However, the technology’s safety promise is tempered by the emergence of algorithmic negligence as a liability.[16]\n\n### Limitations\n\nFor driver monitoring systems, reported performance is highly protocol-dependent, and the lack of standardized benchmarks and cross-dataset validation makes cross-study accuracy comparisons misleading.[14] The shift from driver to vehicle control complicates liability attribution; evidence from aviation and early AV incidents suggests that human operators may become a “moral crumple zone,” blamed even when system design is a contributing factor.[19] The lack of integrated, context-rich data from connected vehicles hinders user-centered evaluation of ADAS; data are often siloed within engineering departments and not readily accessible to design teams.[28] The complexity of AV software, with estimates of up to 300 million lines of code, far exceeds that of aircraft, yet current algorithms often achieve only 95–98% reliability, whereas safety-critical functions may require 99.9999% reliability.[29]\n\nLimitations: The corpus is heavily skewed toward sources of unknown provenance, with 81% of documents lacking a tier classification and only 2% originating from T1 primary studies, limiting confidence in the evidence base. Two high-severity contradictions were detected: sources disagree on accuracy (relative difference 132.6%) and on ttr (relative difference 1342.0%), indicating substantial conflict in key metrics. The evidence horizon spans from 2015-01-01 to the present, which may exclude earlier foundational work.\n\n## Methods\nPre-registered protocol.json (SHA-256 268348acaf5d352c...).\nCorpus: Serper + Semantic Scholar + OpenAlex live retrieval, augmented by domain backends (policy: domain_backends(policy): {'serper_policy': 20}).\nRetrieval fetch outcome: 464 of 740 candidate sources fetched; 276 failed or timed out.\nGenerator model: deepseek/deepseek-v4-pro (multi-section: outline + 10 parallel sections + strict_verify + regen-on-failure).\nEvaluator model: google/gemma-4-31b-it (different family).\nSources classified using T1-T7 tier taxonomy.\nInclusion / exclusion per policy template. 133:
134:Rules:
135:- ENTAILED: every factual assertion in the SENTENCE is supported by the SPAN. Conservative paraphrase is allowed.

137:- CONTRADICTED: the SENTENCE asserts something the SPAN explicitly disagrees with.

140:{{"verdict": "ENTAILED" | "NEUTRAL" | "CONTRADICTED", "reason": "<one short sentence>"}}

145:SENTENCE:
146:{sentence}

169:class _EntailmentJudge:

177:        import httpx  # local import: avoid forcing the dep when off
178:
179:        # Lazy-import the family-segregation check so the off-mode path
180:        # never touches openrouter_client. The judge is acting as a
181:        # content evaluator (Layer-2), so it MUST differ from the
182:        # generator family per CLAUDE.md §9.1.1 — fail at construction
183:        # if PG_ENTAILMENT_MODEL is in the same family as PG_GENERATOR_MODEL.

206:        # variant when PG_GENERATOR_MODEL is also DeepSeek). The

208:        # family from DeepSeek by construction.

210:        self._client = httpx.Client(timeout=_ENTAILMENT_TIMEOUT_S)

125:# I-arch-005 B13 (#1257): the conflict side-judge's EMPTY-CONTENT collapse (a reasoning

 succeeded in 582ms:
1:"""Token-limit resolver (B10 governance — fixes the judge HTTP-400 token-starvation).
2:
3:PROBLEM (operator-locked 2026-06-14, B10 lane):
4:    A role/judge call sets ``max_tokens`` to a large generous budget (correct per
5:    §9.1.8 "go max"), but a model's context window is ``prompt_tokens + max_tokens``.
6:    When ``prompt_tokens + max_tokens`` exceeds the serving provider's context
7:    window (or its per-request completion cap), OpenRouter returns HTTP-400
8:    ("max_tokens ... exceeds context length"). That 400 is exactly what HELD the
9:    A1/A2 qwen-judge report — a generous cap that should have been free insurance
10:    instead aborted the run.
11:
12:THE FIX (a CEILING, never a target):
13:    ``allowed_max_tokens = min(provider_completion_cap,
14:                               context_length - prompt_tokens - safety_margin)``
15:    so ``max_tokens`` NEVER consumes the whole context window. This is a
16:    clamp-DOWN-only ceiling applied as the FINAL mutation of ``body["max_tokens"]``
17:    in ``openrouter_client._call_impl`` (after the §9.1.8 reasoning-first floor/cap
18:    chain). Reasoning EFFORT stays MAX (§9.1.8) — this only bounds the *completion
19:    token allotment* so the request is well-formed.
20:
21:DESIGN INVARIANTS (so the off/offline/generator paths stay byte-identical):
22:    1. PASS-THROUGH when metadata is unavailable. If we cannot resolve a model's
23:       limits (offline, model absent from the /models table, fetch disabled), the
24:       requested ``max_tokens`` is returned UNCHANGED. The generator's 384000
25:       budget on a validated full-cap provider therefore never clamps.
26:    2. CLAMP-DOWN ONLY. ``resolved <= requested`` always; we never RAISE a budget
27:       (raising could re-introduce a different provider's 404/400).
28:    3. FAIL-LOUD, never silent-starve (LAW II / §9.1.8). When the prompt genuinely
29:       does not fit (the prompt alone, plus the safety margin, overruns the context
30:       window so no positive completion budget is well-formed), we RAISE
31:       ``PromptTooLargeError`` and LOUD-log — a tiny max_tokens could not make an
32:       over-window prompt well-formed (the request would still 400), so we surface
33:       the real cause deterministically rather than fire a doomed request or
34:       silently starve. We do NOT re-apply the reasoning-first floor after a clamp
35:       (that would re-create the 400 this module exists to prevent).
36:    4. OFFLINE-SAFE + CACHED. The /api/v1/models table is fetched once, lazily,
37:       behind a TTL cache, via a separately-monkeypatchable fetch hook so tests
38:       INJECT metadata and never touch the network. prompt_tokens is a cheap
39:       char/4 estimate (no heavy tokenizer — §8.4).
40:    5. ENV-GOVERNED (LAW VI). A kill-switch (``PG_TOKEN_LIMIT_RESOLVER``, default
41:       ON) and the safety margin (``PG_TOKEN_LIMIT_SAFETY_MARGIN``) are env knobs.
42:
43:This module is import-safe: it performs NO network I/O at import time.
44:"""
45:
46:from __future__ import annotations
47:
48:import json
49:import logging
50:import os
51:import time
52:import urllib.request
53:from typing import Optional
54:
55:logger = logging.getLogger("polaris.llm.token_limit_resolver")

57:
58:class PromptTooLargeError(ValueError):
59:    """The prompt alone (plus the safety margin) overruns the model's context window.
60:
61:    Raised by compute_allowed_max_tokens when no positive completion budget can
62:    make the request well-formed. This is a deterministic, LOUD prompt-too-large
63:    failure (LAW II / §9.1.8 never-starve) — clamping max_tokens to a tiny value
64:    would NOT help (the prompt still exceeds context → the request still 400s), so
65:    we surface the real cause instead of firing a doomed request or silently
66:    starving. The caller decides how to degrade (e.g. an honest aborted section).
67:    """
68:
69:# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
70:# Env-governed knobs (LAW VI)
71:# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
72:
73:# Master kill-switch. Default ON. When "0", compute_allowed_max_tokens is a
74:# byte-identical pass-through (returns the requested value unchanged) — the
75:# rollback / regression switch the diff-gate can verify.
76:_ENABLED_ENV = "PG_TOKEN_LIMIT_RESOLVER"
77:
78:# Safety margin (tokens) reserved below the context window so prompt + completion
79:# + provider framing overhead never tips into a 400. Generous default; env-tunable.
80:_SAFETY_MARGIN_ENV = "PG_TOKEN_LIMIT_SAFETY_MARGIN"
81:_DEFAULT_SAFETY_MARGIN = 2048
82:
83:# /api/v1/models table cache TTL (seconds). The table is large and changes rarely.
84:_MODELS_TTL_ENV = "PG_TOKEN_LIMIT_MODELS_TTL_SECONDS"
85:_DEFAULT_MODELS_TTL = 3600
86:
87:# Whether the resolver may fetch the live /api/v1/models table at all. Default ON;
88:# set "0" in fully-offline runs to rely solely on the static fallback table.
89:_ALLOW_FETCH_ENV = "PG_TOKEN_LIMIT_ALLOW_FETCH"
90:
91:_MODELS_URL = os.getenv(
92:    "OPENROUTER_BASE_URL", "https://openrouter.ai/api/v1"
93:).rstrip("/") + "/models"

95:
96:def _resolver_enabled() -> bool:
97:    return os.getenv(_ENABLED_ENV, "1") == "1"
98:
99:
100:def _safety_margin() -> int:
101:    try:
102:        return max(0, int(os.getenv(_SAFETY_MARGIN_ENV, str(_DEFAULT_SAFETY_MARGIN))))
103:    except ValueError:
104:        return _DEFAULT_SAFETY_MARGIN

106:
107:# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
108:# Static fallback limits (offline-safe). Conservative, verified figures for the
109:# locked-stack roles so the resolver still bounds a request when the live table
110:# is unreachable. A model ABSENT from both the live table AND this map resolves
111:# to None -> PASS-THROUGH (the requested value is honored unchanged).
112:#
113:# These are APPROXIMATE offline-fallback figures (NOT read from /api/v1/models in
114:# this session — do not treat them as authoritative; the LIVE table is the source
115:# of truth per CLAUDE.md §9.1.8 "read the API, don't guess"). They are chosen
116:# LOWER-bound-safe: a real provider with a bigger window only means we under-clamp
117:# slightly (still well-formed), never over-clamp. When the live table is reachable
118:# it overrides these entirely.
119:#
120:# CAVEAT (advisor B10): the per-model completion cap stored here / read from the
145:    # Judge — the qwen-400 victim. Context window is the binding constraint:
146:    # a large prompt + a generous max_tokens overran it -> HTTP-400.
147:    "qwen/qwen3.6-35b-a3b":       (131072, 65536),
148:}

151:# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
152:# Live /api/v1/models table — lazily fetched, TTL-cached, monkeypatchable.
153:# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
154:
155:# Cache: model_slug -> (context_length, provider_completion_cap_or_None)
156:_models_cache: Optional[dict[str, tuple[int, Optional[int]]]] = None
157:_models_cache_at: float = 0.0

159:
160:def _fetch_models_table() -> Optional[list[dict]]:
161:    """Fetch the raw OpenRouter /api/v1/models ``data`` list.
162:
163:    Returns the list of model dicts, or None on any failure (offline, non-200,
164:    malformed). Tests monkeypatch THIS function to inject metadata without
165:    touching the network. NEVER raises — a fetch failure must degrade to
166:    pass-through, not abort the LLM call.
167:    """
168:    if os.getenv(_ALLOW_FETCH_ENV, "1") != "1":
169:        return None
170:    try:
171:        req = urllib.request.Request(
172:            _MODELS_URL,
173:            headers={"User-Agent": "polaris-token-limit-resolver"},
174:        )
175:        with urllib.request.urlopen(req, timeout=10) as resp:  # nosec B310 (https slug)
176:            if getattr(resp, "status", 200) != 200:
177:                return None
178:            payload = json.loads(resp.read().decode("utf-8"))
179:        data = payload.get("data")
180:        if isinstance(data, list):
181:            return data
182:        return None
183:    except Exception as exc:  # noqa: BLE001 — fetch must never abort the call
184:        logger.warning(
185:            "[token-limit-resolver] /models fetch failed (%s); "
186:            "falling back to static limits / pass-through",
187:            type(exc).__name__,
188:        )
189:        return None
190:
191:
192:def _parse_models_table(data: list[dict]) -> dict[str, tuple[int, Optional[int]]]:
193:    """Reduce the raw /models ``data`` list to {slug: (context_length, completion_cap)}."""
194:    out: dict[str, tuple[int, Optional[int]]] = {}
195:    for entry in data:
196:        try:
197:            slug = entry.get("id")
198:            if not slug:
199:                continue
200:            ctx = entry.get("context_length")
201:            top = entry.get("top_provider") or {}
202:            cap = top.get("max_completion_tokens")
203:            ctx_int = int(ctx) if ctx is not None else None
204:            cap_int = int(cap) if cap is not None else None
205:            if ctx_int is None and cap_int is None:
206:                continue
207:            # If context is unknown but cap is known, treat cap as the bound;
208:            # store a large context so the cap is what binds.
209:            out[slug] = (ctx_int if ctx_int is not None else 10 ** 9, cap_int)
210:        except (TypeError, ValueError):
211:            continue
212:    return out
213:
214:
215:def _get_models_cache() -> dict[str, tuple[int, Optional[int]]]:
216:    """Return the TTL-cached parsed models table (possibly empty)."""
217:    global _models_cache, _models_cache_at
218:    ttl = _DEFAULT_MODELS_TTL
219:    try:
220:        ttl = int(os.getenv(_MODELS_TTL_ENV, str(_DEFAULT_MODELS_TTL)))
221:    except ValueError:
222:        pass
223:    now = time.monotonic()
224:    if _models_cache is not None and (now - _models_cache_at) < ttl:
225:        return _models_cache
226:    raw = _fetch_models_table()
227:    parsed = _parse_models_table(raw) if raw else {}
228:    _models_cache = parsed
229:    _models_cache_at = now
230:    return parsed
300:        (the generator's full-cap budget never clamps).
301:      - the computed ceiling <  requested          -> return the ceiling, LOUD-log.
302:      - the computed ceiling <= 0 (prompt alone overruns the window) -> RAISE
303:        PromptTooLargeError. A tiny max_tokens could NOT make an over-window prompt
304:        well-formed (the request would still 400), so we surface the real cause
305:        loudly and deterministically rather than firing a doomed request or
306:        silently starving (Codex B10 iter-1 P1-3; LAW II / §9.1.8 never-starve).
307:
308:    Reasoning EFFORT is untouched (§9.1.8) — this only bounds completion tokens.
309:    """
310:    if not _resolver_enabled():
311:        return requested_max_tokens
312:    if requested_max_tokens is None or requested_max_tokens <= 0:
313:        return requested_max_tokens
314:
315:    limits = resolve_model_limits(model)
316:    if limits is None:
317:        # Unknown model -> PASS THROUGH (byte-identical to pre-resolver behavior).
318:        return requested_max_tokens
319:
320:    context_length, completion_cap = limits
321:    margin = _safety_margin()
322:
323:    ceiling_candidates: list[int] = []
324:    if context_length is not None:
325:        ceiling_candidates.append(int(context_length) - int(prompt_tokens) - margin)
326:    if completion_cap is not None and apply_completion_cap:
327:        ceiling_candidates.append(int(completion_cap))
328:
329:    if not ceiling_candidates:
330:        return requested_max_tokens
331:
332:    ceiling = min(ceiling_candidates)
333:
334:    if ceiling >= requested_max_tokens:
335:        # Request already fits -> no clamp. Generator full-cap path lands here.
336:        return requested_max_tokens
337:
338:    if ceiling <= 0:
339:        # The prompt alone (plus margin) overruns the window. Clamping max_tokens to
340:        # a tiny value would NOT make the request well-formed — the prompt still
341:        # exceeds context, so OpenRouter would still 400 (or, if accepted, the model
342:        # silently starves). RAISE a deterministic, LOUD prompt-too-large failure so
343:        # the genuine cause surfaces (Codex B10 iter-1 P1-3; LAW II fail-loud).
344:        msg = (
345:            f"prompt too large for model={model}: prompt~{prompt_tokens} tokens + "
346:            f"safety_margin {margin} >= context_length {context_length} "
347:            f"(completion_cap={completion_cap}); no positive completion budget fits. "
348:            f"Reduce the prompt or use a larger-context model. "
349:            f"(requested max_tokens was {requested_max_tokens}.)"
350:        )
351:        logger.error("[token-limit-resolver] FAIL-LOUD: %s", msg)
352:        raise PromptTooLargeError(msg)
353:
354:    # Genuine clamp-down: the generous request would overrun the window.
355:    logger.warning(
356:        "[token-limit-resolver] clamping max_tokens %d -> %d for model=%s "
357:        "(context_length=%s, completion_cap=%s, prompt~%d tokens, margin=%d). "
358:        "Reasoning effort unchanged; this prevents the HTTP-400 context overrun "
359:        "(the qwen-judge 400 that held the report).",
360:        requested_max_tokens, ceiling, model, context_length, completion_cap,
361:        prompt_tokens, margin,
362:    )

233:def reset_cache() -> None:
234:    """Clear the models cache (test hook)."""

236:    _models_cache = None

239:
240:def resolve_model_limits(model: str) -> Optional[tuple[int, Optional[int]]]:
241:    """Return (context_length, provider_completion_cap_or_None) for ``model``.
242:
243:    Resolution order: live /models table -> static fallback -> None.
244:    None means "unknown" -> the caller must PASS THROUGH the requested budget.
245:    """
246:    if not model:
247:        return None
248:    table = _get_models_cache()
249:    if model in table:
250:        return table[model]
251:    if model in _STATIC_MODEL_LIMITS:
252:        return _STATIC_MODEL_LIMITS[model]
253:    return None
254:
255:
256:def estimate_prompt_tokens(messages: list[dict]) -> int:
257:    """Cheap char/4 prompt-token estimate (no heavy tokenizer — §8.4).
258:
259:    Deliberately conservative-high: counts role + content chars so we slightly
260:    OVER-estimate the prompt, which makes the ceiling slightly SMALLER — the safe
261:    direction (a 400 is worse than a marginally shorter completion).
262:    """
263:    total_chars = 0
264:    for msg in messages or []:
265:        content = msg.get("content")
266:        if isinstance(content, str):
267:            total_chars += len(content)
268:        elif content is not None:
269:            total_chars += len(str(content))
270:        # small fixed overhead per message for role/framing tokens
271:        total_chars += 8
272:    return (total_chars // 4) + 1
273:
274:
275:def compute_allowed_max_tokens(
276:    model: str,
277:    prompt_tokens: int,
278:    requested_max_tokens: int,
279:    apply_completion_cap: bool = True,
280:) -> int:
281:    """Clamp ``requested_max_tokens`` so prompt + completion fits the context window.
282:
283:    Returns ``min(requested_max_tokens, provider_completion_cap,
284:                  context_length - prompt_tokens - safety_margin)``.
285:
286:    ``apply_completion_cap`` (default True): when False, the ``top_provider``
287:    completion-cap candidate is IGNORED and only the ``context_length`` bound
288:    applies. The caller sets this False for the PROVIDER-PINNED generator family
289:    (``_REASONING_FIRST_MODELS``): #1253 pins that family to full-cap providers
290:    (Novita/WandB >= 384k) and EXCLUDES DeepInfra, but OpenRouter's
291:    ``top_provider.max_completion_tokens`` is a popularity-ranked field that may
292:    report DeepInfra's 16384 — applying it would clamp a 32k-64k section request
293:    back down to 16384, re-introducing the exact #1253 starvation. The judge/
294:    verifier roles are NOT reasoning-first, so their completion-cap clamp (the
295:    actual HTTP-400 fix) stays intact (Codex/advisor B10 online-path finding).
296:
297:    CLAMP-DOWN-ONLY + PASS-THROUGH semantics:
298:      - resolver disabled OR model limits unknown  -> return requested unchanged.
299:      - the computed ceiling >= requested          -> return requested unchanged

121:# live table is OpenRouter's ``top_provider.max_completion_tokens`` — a
122:# popularity-ranked field, NOT necessarily the GENUINELY-SERVED provider's cap.
123:# The spec's "overlay the serving provider cap" is therefore only partially met:
124:# the caller compensates for the one case where it matters (the provider-pinned
125:# reasoning-first generator family) via ``apply_completion_cap=False``. Resolving
126:# the true served-provider cap per /models/<id>/endpoints is a residual refinement.
127:# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------

137:# the genuine binding constraint (the qwen-judge 400 this module fixes). When the

403:# cheap 90s shared default (which also governs retrieval/embeddings). Env-overridable (LAW VI).
404:# NOTE: this is the PER-POST httpx timeout (bounds ONE network call). It does NOT bound the COMPOSED
405:# retry loop (effort-ladder x transport-retries x rate-limit-backoff), which can stack to hours per
406:# claim — that composition is bounded SEPARATELY by the #1226 wall-clock watchdog below.
407:_TIMEOUT_SECONDS = int(os.getenv("PG_VERIFIER_LLM_TIMEOUT_SECONDS", "900"))
408:
409:# === #1226 (I-pipe-001): blank-content bound + per-call wall-clock watchdog =====================
410:# THE FAILURE: a reasoning verifier (e.g. GLM-5.1 Mirror under xhigh) can burn its whole token
411:# budget on reasoning and emit EMPTY content every attempt; combined with the per-POST 900s timeout
412:# (_TIMEOUT_SECONDS) the COMPOSED retry loop (effort-ladder/provider-failover x transport-retries x
413:# rate-limit-backoff) can stall for HOURS per claim, so the D8 4-role gate never finishes and the
414:# whole sweep hangs. Two RELIABILITY backstops (NEVER touch a verify/NLI/4-role verdict; on
415:# exhaustion they re-raise the EXISTING fail-loud BlankVerdictError/RoleTransportError -> release
416:# stays HELD; no fake/empty verdict is ever synthesized):
417:#
418:#   (1) PG_ROLE_BLANK_MAX_RETRIES (default 3): a HARD ceiling on the number of blank-content RETRIES.
419:#       Implemented by TRUNCATING the per-call effort/provider ladder to `1 + max_retries` attempts.
420:#       At the default (1 + 3 = 4) this ceiling is >= every role's existing default ladder length
421:#       (Judge 3, Mirror/routed-Sentinel 1+PG_PROVIDER_BLANK_RETRIES=4, classifier-Sentinel 1,
422:#       decomposition-Sentinel 3), so the truncation is the IDENTITY at default — it only bites when
423:#       a custom PG_PROVIDER_BLANK_RETRIES / PG_FOUR_ROLE_EFFORT_LADDER pushes a ladder past the cap.
424:#       Exhaustion fires the SAME existing `raise` at ladder-end -> the existing BlankVerdictError.
425:#
426:#   (2) PG_ROLE_CALL_TIMEOUT_S (default 3600s/1h): a COOPERATIVE wall-clock watchdog over the whole
427:#       `complete()` retry loop (checked at the top of each attempt + before each rate-limit sleep).
428:#       It does NOT interrupt an in-flight POST (httpx's _TIMEOUT_SECONDS bounds that) — it bounds the
429:#       COMPOSITION so a stuck-in-retries call aborts LOUDLY instead of hanging the sweep. 1h is a
430:#       deliberately-generous BACKSTOP against a wedged call, NOT a tight SLO: it sits well above any
431:#       HEALTHY 4-role call (a healthy claim resolves in seconds-to-minutes — the ladder rarely runs
432:#       to exhaustion, and a single blanking call returns its empty 200 quickly, it does not consume
433:#       the full per-POST 900s). The worst-case LEGITIMATE composition (every rung waiting near the
434:#       full _TIMEOUT_SECONDS, plus transport-retries and rate-limit backoff) CAN approach/exceed 1h;
435:#       that path is already pathological (it IS the stall this fix targets), so aborting it loudly is
436:#       the intended behavior, and the operator RAISES PG_ROLE_CALL_TIMEOUT_S if a slower legitimate
437:#       budget is ever needed (LAW VI). On trip it re-raises the last BlankVerdictError (if the loop
438:#       was blanking) else a RoleTransportError (both fail-loud; never a synthesized verdict).
439:#
440:# KILL-SWITCH (default-ON correct fix): PG_ROLE_BLANK_WATCHDOG (default "1"). Set "0"/"false"/"no" to
441:# REVERT to the prior behavior — when OFF neither the ladder truncation nor the watchdog check ever
442:# fires, so control flow is byte-identical to before #1226. (Reliability-only kill-switch pattern.)
443:# Read LAZILY per-call (mirrors the adjacent PG_* knobs) so an override set after import is honored.
444:_ROLE_BLANK_WATCHDOG_ENV = "PG_ROLE_BLANK_WATCHDOG"
445:_ROLE_BLANK_MAX_RETRIES_ENV = "PG_ROLE_BLANK_MAX_RETRIES"
446:_ROLE_BLANK_MAX_RETRIES_DEFAULT = "3"
447:_ROLE_CALL_TIMEOUT_S_ENV = "PG_ROLE_CALL_TIMEOUT_S"
448:_ROLE_CALL_TIMEOUT_S_DEFAULT = "3600.0"
449:

455:    control flow); absent or anything else -> ON (the default-ON correct fix). LAW VI: env-driven,

280:# I-run11-004: hard floor on the decomposition Sentinel's top-level max_tokens. The certified
281:# MiniMax-M2 call used reasoning + max_tokens>=3000; anything below that truncates the JSON
282:# {verdict, atoms} mid-emission (the run-12 truncator) and collapses every claim to a fail-closed
283:# UNGROUNDED. An env override (PG_SENTINEL_DECOMPOSITION_MAX_TOKENS) can RAISE but never lower past
284:# this floor.
285:_SENTINEL_DECOMPOSITION_MIN_MAX_TOKENS = 3000

287:# I-arch-003 (#1253) hardening (Codex gate P2): per-role CHAIN-MIN CEILING on the resolved total
288:# max_tokens (and the Mirror numeric reasoning cap). Under provider allow_fallbacks:false the binding
289:# output cap is the MIN max_completion_tokens across the role's pinned provider chain (live OpenRouter
290:# /endpoints read 2026-06-14). A budget that EXCEEDS this — whether the default or a future bad env
291:# override — would hard-400 ("requested N > max M") on the chain; that is exactly the failure class this
292:# audit closes. So every resolved budget is clamped DOWN to its chain min (env can lower for cost/testing
293:# but never raise past the provider cap). RE-DERIVE these if a role's chain in
294:# config/settings/openrouter_provider_routing.yaml is re-pinned to higher-cap-only providers.
295:_MIRROR_MAX_TOKENS_CHAIN_MIN = 131072    # glm-5.1: atlas-cloud 202752, z-ai/baidu/novita/gmicloud >= 131072
296:_SENTINEL_MAX_TOKENS_CHAIN_MIN = 131072  # minimax-m2: google/atlas-cloud 196608, novita/minimax 131072
297:_JUDGE_MAX_TOKENS_CHAIN_MIN = 262140     # qwen3.6: wandb 262144, io-net 262140
298:
299:# I-meta-008 / #1026: blank-verdict step-down ladder. When a reasoning-first verifier returns a
300:# BLANK bare verdict (reasoning budget exhausted without converging under the high effort), retry
301:# with the effort stepped DOWN this ladder so the model is forced to spend tokens on the VERDICT,
302:# not endless reasoning. The first entry is the configured MAX effort; `None` means reasoning
303:# DISABLED (final resort — guarantees content). Overridable via PG_FOUR_ROLE_EFFORT_LADDER
304:# (comma-separated; the literal "off"/"none" -> reasoning disabled). MAX-reasoning-first, then a
305:# guaranteed verdict, then fail-loud only if even reasoning-off blanks.
306:def _parse_effort_ladder() -> tuple[object, ...]:
307:    raw = os.getenv("PG_FOUR_ROLE_EFFORT_LADDER")
308:    if not raw:
309:        return (_REASONING_EFFORT, "low", None)
310:    out: list[object] = []
311:    for token in raw.split(","):
312:        t = token.strip()
313:        if not t:
314:            continue
315:        out.append(None if t.lower() in ("off", "none", "disabled") else t)
316:    return tuple(out) if out else (_REASONING_EFFORT, "low", None)

330:# max_tokens>=3000 — turning reasoning OFF (or starving max_tokens to the 256 classifier budget)
331:# truncates the JSON {verdict, atoms} mid-emission and collapses every claim to a fail-closed
332:# UNGROUNDED (the run-12 truncator). So when the active Sentinel groundedness mode is
333:# "decomposition", the Sentinel sends MAX reasoning + a GENEROUS max_tokens, exactly replicating
334:# the certified call. The SOVEREIGN `guardian`/`noninverted` granite Sentinel modes stay
335:# reasoning-OFF (a <score>/one-word classifier whose self-host slug does not advertise
336:# `reasoning`); their settings are NOT regressed. LAW VI: overridable via `PG_SENTINEL_REASONING`.
337:# I-run11-008 (#1053): Mirror keeps reasoning ON (bake-off: reasoning-OFF DOUBLED false_bind 1->2/5,
338:# i.e. a worse grounding judge), but its reasoning is BOUNDED numerically below (effort=xhigh is a
339:# no-op on GLM, so unbounded thinking exhausted max_tokens → the 47 blanks). Overridable via PG_MIRROR_REASONING.
340:_ROLE_REASONING_DEFAULT = {"mirror": True, "sentinel": False, "judge": True}

343:def sentinel_decomposition_active(slug_override: str | None = None) -> bool:

505:
506:def _parse_retry_after_seconds(value: str | None) -> float | None:
507:    """Parse the `Retry-After` header as integer seconds (RFC 9110 delta-seconds form).
508:
509:    Returns the non-negative float seconds on success, or `None` when the header is absent,
510:    empty, or a non-numeric/HTTP-date form — in which case the caller falls back to the capped
511:    exponential backoff. We deliberately do NOT parse the HTTP-date form (per the I-beatboth-429
512:    spec): any non-integer value is treated as the absent case so a malformed header can never
513:    yield a negative or unbounded sleep.
514:    """
515:    if value is None:
516:        return None
517:    try:
518:        seconds = int(value.strip())
519:    except (ValueError, AttributeError):
520:        return None
521:    return float(seconds) if seconds >= 0 else None

See manifest.frame_coverage_report and human_gap_tasks.json for per-entity detail.[4]\n\n### Long-term Outcomes\n\nThe Movement Disorder Society-sponsored revision of the Unified Parkinson's Disease Rating Scale (MDS-UPDRS), validated in a study of 877 patients from 39 sites, demonstrated high internal consistency (Cronbach's alpha = 0.79–0.93 across parts) and strong correlation with the original UPDRS (ρ = 0.96), providing a comprehensive framework for tracking these changes across four parts: non-motor experiences of daily living, motor experiences of daily living, motor examination, and motor complications.[3] In advanced stages, symptoms are severe and daily life usually requires full-time support.[5]\n\n### Safety\n\nThe FDA labeling for carbidopa-levodopa explicitly warns that patients should be observed carefully when the dosage is reduced abruptly or discontinued, especially if they are receiving neuroleptics.[6] DBS hardware complications are relatively common; in a prospective series of 81 consecutive patients with PD undergoing subthalamic nucleus (STN) DBS followed for a mean of 17 months, hardware complications occurred in 26.2% of patients (atom_009).[2] Specific complications included infections requiring system removal in 2.5% (atom_010) and implantable pulse generator (IPG) removal in 3.7% (atom_011), misplaced leads in 12.5% (atom_012), and lead migration, fracture, or IPG malfunction.[2] A review of the FDA MAUDE database over 10 years identified 221 unique adverse events, with infection being the most common (16.2%) (atom_013) and lead migration accounting for 8.6% (atom_014).[7] Over a third (40%) of all devices with adverse events required return to the operating room for explant or revision (atom_003, atom_004).[7] A review of postoperative psychosis after DBS reported a lifetime incidence of psychosis of 28.3% (atom_018) in PD patients following DBS, underscoring the need for vigilance.[8] Additionally, implantable pulse generator expiration can precipitate a movement disorder emergency with severe parkinsonian worsening or akinetic crisis, necessitating urgent medical evaluation.[9] Abrupt reduction or discontinuation of carbidopa-levodopa has been associated with a symptom complex resembling neuroleptic malignant syndrome (NMS), characterized by hyperthermia, muscle rigidity, altered consciousness, and autonomic dysfunction.[6] Psychosis can emerge after STN DBS, as illustrated by clinical cases where patients developed paranoid ideation, hallucinations, and delusions within months of surgery, requiring antipsychotic treatment.[8] Patients taking dopaminergic medications may experience intense urges to gamble, increased sexual urges, binge eating, and other compulsive behaviors, which may resolve with dose reduction or discontinuation[6]\n\n### Population Subgroups\n\nThe choice between rechargeable and non‑rechargeable IPGs was significantly associated with affordability (χ²=19.13, p<0.001), and remote programming capability influenced brand selection.[10] Despite this, a preliminary survey of 17 DBS patients (85% of those approached) found that 76.5% used their patient programmer once per month or less, 17.7% never used it, and the most common function was checking battery life (58.8%).[11] Adaptive DBS shows promise, as one study reported that overall well‑being significantly improved with adaptive stimulation (p=0.007).[12] Caregivers of intermediate PD spent 6.6 hours per day caring, compared to 3.2 hours for early PD, and were less likely to be employed (25.3% vs 42.4%).[13] Personalized DBS programming using machine learning to optimize gait has produced meaningful improvements in walking, addressing a common post‑DBS challenge.[14] Remote patient monitoring devices led 79.9% of participants to agree that they improved mobility, and 48% perceived themselves as safer.[15] However, a meta‑synthesis of wearable device experiences identified barriers including discomfort, emotional distress, and interface difficulties that hinder long‑term adherence.[16] 87.3% of DBS patients used rechargeable IPGs (r-IPGs), 87.7% were satisfied with their device; among those with r-IPGs, 40.6% felt confident handling the device within one week of discharge, 71.4% recharged independently, and the mean recharge interval was 4.3 days.[10] Caregivers of people with advanced PD had significantly higher perceived burden (ZBI 34.5 vs 24.7 for early PD) and were more likely to take medication due to caregiving (34.7% vs 13.8%).[13] Assistive technologies also contribute: a scoping review reported that home automation significantly increased quality of life (p<0.001), and 63.5% of participants used speech‑to‑text functions to cope with tremors.[15]\n\n### Limitations\n\nLimitations: The pipeline's tier distribution reveals a heavy reliance on lower-quality evidence, with only 10% of sources classified as T1 primary studies and a striking 70% falling into the UNKNOWN tier. Six high-severity contradictions were detected, including a 972.4% relative difference in mortality estimates and a 481.0% divergence in hazard ratio, indicating sources disagree sharply on both magnitude and direction of key outcomes. The evidence horizon spans from 2010-01-01 to the present, but the predominance of unclassified sources and sparse representation in tiers T2 (1%), T3 (1%), and T5 (0%) further limit confidence in the synthesized findings.\n\n## Methods\nPre-registered protocol.json (SHA-256 36a59dc009aa949f...).\nCorpus: Serper + Semantic Scholar + OpenAlex live retrieval, augmented by domain backends (clinical: domain_backends(clinical): {'europe_pmc': 27}).\nRetrieval fetch outcome: 478 of 740 candidate sources fetched; 262 failed or timed out.\nGenerator model: deepseek/deepseek-v4-pro (multi-section: outline + 6 parallel sections + strict_verify + regen-on-failure).\nEvaluator model: google/gemma-4-31b-it (different family).\nSources classified using T1-T7 tier taxonomy.\nInclusion / exclusion per clinical template. tests\fixtures\redaction_fixture_redact001.json:1160:      "report_text": "## Key Findings\n\n_Each finding below is a verbatim, span-verified statement carried up from the body section named in bold; citations are the body's._\n\n- **Technical_Standard.** ### SAE J3016 driving-automation levels and the human-machine boundary\n\nLevel 5:\nFull Driving Automation.[1]\n- **Legal_Framework.** 157 - Automated Lane Keeping Systems (ALKS).[2]\n- **Background.** The Society of Automotive Engineers (SAE) International standard J3016_202104 defines six levels of driving automation, ranging from Level 0 (no driving automation) to Level 5 (full driving automation), and establishes a taxonomy for on-road motor vehicle driving automation systems.[1]\n- **Key Findings.** In the European Union, a 2025 European Parliament study advocates for a strict liability regime targeting high-risk AI systems, structured around a single responsible operator to avoid regulatory fragmentation among Member States.[15]\n- **Evidence and Analysis.** The EU’s revised Product Liability Directive explicitly extends to software and AI-driven products, including autonomous vehicles, and introduces presumptions of defect and causation to ease the burden on injured parties.[15][24]\n- **Comparative Assessment.** A key challenge across jurisdictions is allocating liability when semi-autonomous systems require constant human oversight, as drivers may over-rely on automation, leading to mode confusion and delayed re-engagement.[17][9]\n\n### SAE J3016 driving-automation levels and the human-machine boundary\n\nLevel 5:\nFull Driving Automation.[1] Standard id: J3016_202104.[1] The SAE J3016_202104 standard defines six levels of driving automation.[1] Level 0 is No Driving Automation.[1] In contrast, Level 1 is Driver Assistance.[1] Level 0 and Level 1 are No Driving Automation and Driver Assistance, respectively.[1] Finally, Level 5 is Full Driving Automation.[1] These are No Driving Automation, Driver Assistance, Partial Driving Automation, Conditional Driving Automation, High Driving Automation, and Full Driving Automation, respectively.[1] Although Level 0 is No Driving Automation, Level 1 is Driver Assistance.[1] In contrast, Level 2 is Partial Driving Automation, whereas Level 3 is Conditional Driving Automation.[1] The taxonomy's six levels are No Driving Automation, Driver Assistance, Partial Driving Automation, Conditional Driving Automation, High Driving Automation, and Full Driving Automation.[1]\n\n### UNECE WP.29 ALKS regulation (system-vs-driver responsibility)\n\nInstrument: UN Regulation No. 157 - Automated Lane Keeping Systems (ALKS).[2] UN Regulation No. 157 - Automated Lane Keeping Systems (ALKS) is the instrument identified in the primary source.[2] The subject matter is Automated Lane Keeping Systems.[2] In contrast, the regulation’s title clearly indicates its focus on automated lane keeping.[2] The regulation’s full designation is UN Regulation No. 157 - Automated Lane Keeping Systems (ALKS).[2] The regulation, UN Regulation No. 157, addresses Automated Lane Keeping Systems.[2]\n\n### NHTSA automated-driving-systems policy and crash-reporting order\n\nScope: Entities named in the General Order.[3] Issuing agency: NHTSA.[3] The scope of this order is precisely defined, applying exclusively to entities named in the General Order.[3] However, the reporting obligation is not triggered for every crash involving those entities; it is conditional upon specific temporal and outcome-based criteria.[3] Alternatively, the obligation arises if the crash involved a vulnerable road user being struck.[3] In contrast, a crash that does not meet these consequence thresholds, even if ADS was active within the 30-second window, would not require reporting under the order.[3] The reporting criteria also encompass an air bag deployment.[3] Finally, any individual being transported to a hospital for medical treatment as a result of the crash mandates a report.[3] By comparison, crashes where ADS was not in use within 30 seconds, or where the damage is below the specified property damage threshold, fall outside the mandatory reporting scope.[3]\n\n### Product-liability / negligence doctrine for ADAS manufacturers\n\nDefect categories: manufacturing defects; design defects; and inadequate instructions or warnings defects.[4]\n\n### Relevant ADAS / automated-feature crash-liability case law\n\nContract-bound content for adas_crash_case_law did not survive strict verification against retrieved primary source text; this slot is a curator-actionable gap. See manifest.frame_coverage_report and human_gap_tasks.json for per-entity detail.[5]\n\n### Proposed regulatory guidelines and liability-allocation recommendations\n\nContract-bound content for proposed_regulatory_guidelines did not survive strict verification against retrieved primary source text; this slot is a curator-actionable gap. See manifest.frame_coverage_report and human_gap_tasks.json for per-entity detail.[6]\n\n### Background\n\nThe Society of Automotive Engineers (SAE) International standard J3016_202104 defines six levels of driving automation, ranging from Level 0 (no driving automation) to Level 5 (full driving automation), and establishes a taxonomy for on-road motor vehicle driving automation systems.[1] Active safety systems such as electronic stability control (ESC) and automatic emergency braking (AEB) are excluded from this taxonomy because they provide only momentary intervention rather than sustained performance of the dynamic driving task (DDT).[1] Common ADAS functions include adaptive cruise control (ACC), blind spot detection (BSD), lane departure warning/lane keeping systems (LDW/LKS), intelligent headlamp control (IHC), and emergency brake assist (EBA).[7] Large-scale crash statistics indicate that among 7.7 million vehicles manufactured between 2015 and 2023, ADAS-equipped cars experienced on average a 27% reduction in forward-collision crashes and an 11% reduction in lane-departure crashes.[8] Blind Spot Monitoring (BSM) systems are estimated to reduce lane-change crashes by 14% and lane-change crashes with injuries by 23%.[9] For example, lane departure warning (LDW) systems were found to be turned off in 67.2% of observed vehicles due to lack of trust and excessive false alarms.[10] Camera-based DMS have been shown to detect driver distraction approximately 50 seconds sooner than steering-wheel-based DMS, and users of steering-wheel DMS were able to engage in 5.65 minutes of continuous distracted driving before an alert, compared to 2.25 minutes for camera-based DMS.[11] The increasing complexity of ADAS and automated driving systems has prompted the development of specialized accident investigation frameworks, such as a comprehensive system proposed in China that integrates traffic accident boundary analysis, control action safety analysis, and causal scene restoration.[12] To evaluate ADAS safety effectiveness, researchers have identified 94 influence parameters from sources including published literature, standardized tests, accident databases, and autonomous vehicle disengagement reports, with 77 parameters assigned importance levels through cluster analysis.[13] Driver drowsiness remains a significant safety concern, with the World Health Organization reporting approximately 1.35 million traffic-related deaths annually, and research indicating that steering wheel angle variability increases from 0.5–1.5° in alert drivers to 2–5° in fatigued states.[14] If the driver does not fully understand the capabilities and limitations of the system, this might lead to mode confusion and can cause surprises for the driver.[9] In the European Union, several SAE Level 0 ADAS will be mandatory by 2022, while no automated driving technology is yet permitted on the EU market.[9]\n\n### Key Findings\n\nIn the European Union, a 2025 European Parliament study advocates for a strict liability regime targeting high-risk AI systems, structured around a single responsible operator to avoid regulatory fragmentation among Member States.[15] This verdict signals a shift toward manufacturer accountability for AI driving systems.[16] NHTSA investigations have identified patterns of failures in Tesla's ADAS, including a probe of 2.4 million vehicles for crashes at intersections and emergency vehicle collisions, and a subsequent probe of 2.88 million vehicles involving 58 traffic violation reports, 14 crashes, and 23 injuries.[16] One analysis argues that semi-autonomous systems create a collaborative driving endeavor, and that automakers' marketing efforts to gain public trust may render them primarily liable when that trust is misplaced, with a reduction for the driver's comparative fault.[17] The opacity of AI decision-making and the 'problem of many hands'—diffusion of responsibility among multiple actors—create a liability gap that traditional tort frameworks struggle to address.[18] A growing body of policy analysis advocates for strict liability for high-risk AI systems, with proposals ranging from a single responsible operator under EU law to no-fault insurance schemes.[15].[18][19] One scholar proposes extending strict products liability to defective software components in autonomous vehicles, treating software creators as strictly liable for injuries caused by dangerously defective code.[20] A law and economics analysis suggests that negligence-based rules can efficiently accommodate AV-human interactions, but require substantial doctrinal reforms, including reconceptualization of fault standards for both AVs and human drivers.[21] Jurisdictional approaches vary: Michigan's product liability statutes govern claims involving defective ADAS, Louisiana applies a comparative fault system to apportion liability between driver and manufacturer, and the UK's Automated and Electric Vehicles Act 2018 explicitly addresses liability for accidents resulting from unauthorized software alterations or failure to update software.[22][23][16]\n\n### Evidence and Analysis\n\nThe EU’s revised Product Liability Directive explicitly extends to software and AI-driven products, including autonomous vehicles, and introduces presumptions of defect and causation to ease the burden on injured parties.[15][24] In China, the undefined legal status of autonomous driving systems has prompted the development of a comprehensive accident investigation system that integrates boundary analysis, control action safety assessment, and causal scene restoration to allocate responsibility.[12] US legal scholarship argues that automakers’ marketing of ADAS features fosters driver trust, and when that trust is misplaced, products liability law supports primary manufacturer liability with a potential reduction for the driver’s comparative fault, rather than placing full responsibility on the human operator.[17] The revised PLD is an EU instrument for AI-related harm[15][24]\n\n### Comparative Assessment\n\nA key challenge across jurisdictions is allocating liability when semi-autonomous systems require constant human oversight, as drivers may over-rely on automation, leading to mode confusion and delayed re-engagement.[17][9] Under U.S. products liability law, automakers may face primary liability for ADAS failures, though the driver's comparative fault can reduce recovery[17][22] Legal scholars debate whether the human driver or the manufacturer bears responsibility when supervised autonomy fails in semi-autonomous vehicles.[17]\n\n### Implications\n\nIn the United Kingdom, the Automated and Electric Vehicles Act 2018 addresses liability for accidents caused by automated vehicles, including those resulting from unauthorized software alterations or failure to update software.[16] Texas has legislatively limited lessors’ liability for failing to retrofit optional safety systems, provided the vehicle met federal standards at purchase.[25] Empirical evidence from a survey of 260 drivers indicates that collision avoidance performance and system reliability are the strongest predictors of user trust, while road safety impact emerges as an independent societal benefit.[26] On the cost side, an Italian analysis estimates that an average ADAS investment of €1,629 per vehicle yields nationwide savings of €1.89 billion in 2024, with most systems becoming cost-effective within two to three years.[27] Daimler Trucks North America was granted summary judgment in a lawsuit.[25] However, the technology’s safety promise is tempered by the emergence of algorithmic negligence as a liability.[16]\n\n### Limitations\n\nFor driver monitoring systems, reported performance is highly protocol-dependent, and the lack of standardized benchmarks and cross-dataset validation makes cross-study accuracy comparisons misleading.[14] The shift from driver to vehicle control complicates liability attribution; evidence from aviation and early AV incidents suggests that human operators may become a “moral crumple zone,” blamed even when system design is a contributing factor.[19] The lack of integrated, context-rich data from connected vehicles hinders user-centered evaluation of ADAS; data are often siloed within engineering departments and not readily accessible to design teams.[28] The complexity of AV software, with estimates of up to 300 million lines of code, far exceeds that of aircraft, yet current algorithms often achieve only 95–98% reliability, whereas safety-critical functions may require 99.9999% reliability.[29]\n\nLimitations: The corpus is heavily skewed toward sources of unknown provenance, with 81% of documents lacking a tier classification and only 2% originating from T1 primary studies, limiting confidence in the evidence base. Two high-severity contradictions were detected: sources disagree on accuracy (relative difference 132.6%) and on ttr (relative difference 1342.0%), indicating substantial conflict in key metrics. The evidence horizon spans from 2015-01-01 to the present, which may exclude earlier foundational work.\n\n## Methods\nPre-registered protocol.json (SHA-256 268348acaf5d352c...).\nCorpus: Serper + Semantic Scholar + OpenAlex live retrieval, augmented by domain backends (policy: domain_backends(policy): {'serper_policy': 20}).\nRetrieval fetch outcome: 464 of 740 candidate sources fetched; 276 failed or timed out.\nGenerator model: deepseek/deepseek-v4-pro (multi-section: outline + 10 parallel sections + strict_verify + regen-on-failure).\nEvaluator model: google/gemma-4-31b-it (different family).\nSources classified using T1-T7 tier taxonomy.\nInclusion / exclusion per policy template. 77:def _int_env(name: str, default: int) -> int:
78:    try:
79:        value = int(os.environ.get(name, "") or default)
80:    except (TypeError, ValueError):
81:        return default
82:    return value if value > 0 else default

86:    try:
87:        value = float(os.environ.get(name, "") or default)
88:    except (TypeError, ValueError):
89:        return default
90:    return value if value > 0 else default

680:    """Fail fast if generator and evaluator are in the same family.
681:
682:    Returns (generator_family, evaluator_family) on success.
683:    Raises RuntimeError if same family or if either is "unknown" without
684:    an explicit override (so misconfiguration is visible at init time).
685:
686:    Defaults pull from the module-level env-var settings.
687:    """
688:    gen_model = generator_model or PG_GENERATOR_MODEL
689:    eval_model = evaluator_model or PG_EVALUATOR_MODEL
690:    gen_override = generator_override if generator_override is not None else PG_GENERATOR_FAMILY_OVERRIDE
691:    eval_override = evaluator_override if evaluator_override is not None else PG_EVALUATOR_FAMILY_OVERRIDE
692:
693:    gen_family = family_from_model(gen_model, gen_override)
694:    eval_family = family_from_model(eval_model, eval_override)
695:
696:    if gen_family == "unknown" and not gen_override:
697:        raise RuntimeError(
698:            f"HONEST-REBUILD Phase 1c: generator model '{gen_model}' does not "
699:            f"match any known family prefix. Set PG_GENERATOR_FAMILY_OVERRIDE "
700:            f"to the training-lineage family (e.g. 'deepseek', 'qwen') so "
701:            f"family segregation can be checked. Family-distance from the "
702:            f"evaluator is the primary mitigation for evaluator-generator "
703:            f"self-bias (Play Favorites arXiv:2508.06709, DeepHalluBench "
704:            f"arXiv:2601.22984)."
705:        )
706:    if eval_family == "unknown" and not eval_override:
707:        raise RuntimeError(
708:            f"HONEST-REBUILD Phase 1c: evaluator model '{eval_model}' does "
709:            f"not match any known family prefix. Set PG_EVALUATOR_FAMILY_OVERRIDE."
710:        )

712:        raise RuntimeError(
713:            f"HONEST-REBUILD Phase 1c: generator and evaluator are in the "
714:            f"same training-lineage family ('{gen_family}'). This defeats "
715:            f"the purpose of two-family architecture — same-family judges "
716:            f"share blind spots and RLHF biases. Pick models from different "
717:            f"families, or document the choice with explicit PG_*_FAMILY_OVERRIDE "
718:            f"values to demonstrate you know the trade-off. Recommended pair: "
719:            f"deepseek/deepseek-v4-pro (generator) + google/gemma-4-31b-it "
720:            f"(evaluator) per loopback/audit/_open_source_models_2026.md."
721:        )
722:    return (gen_family, eval_family)

100:def _safety_margin() -> int:
120:# CAVEAT (advisor B10): the per-model completion cap stored here / read from the
121:# live table is OpenRouter's ``top_provider.max_completion_tokens`` — a
122:# popularity-ranked field, NOT necessarily the GENUINELY-SERVED provider's cap.
123:# The spec's "overlay the serving provider cap" is therefore only partially met:
124:# the caller compensates for the one case where it matters (the provider-pinned
125:# reasoning-first generator family) via ``apply_completion_cap=False``. Resolving
126:# the true served-provider cap per /models/<id>/endpoints is a residual refinement.
127:# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
128:
129:# model_slug -> (context_length, provider_completion_cap_or_None)
130:#
131:# Codex B10 iter-1 P1-2: the GENERATOR is DELIBERATELY ABSENT from this static
132:# table. The generator runs on the validated full-cap fp8 chain at the §9.1.8
133:# 384000 budget; a conservative static context here would clamp that 384000 DOWN
134:# offline (a #1253 regression). Absent -> resolve_model_limits returns None ->
135:# PASS-THROUGH (byte-identical), which is exactly right for the generator. The
136:# static fallback covers ONLY the verifier/judge roles whose smaller windows are
137:# the genuine binding constraint (the qwen-judge 400 this module fixes). When the
138:# live /models table IS reachable it supplies the generator's true (large) limits,
139:# which also never clamp the 384000 request.
140:_STATIC_MODEL_LIMITS: dict[str, tuple[int, Optional[int]]] = {
141:    # Mirror / evaluator / side-judges.
142:    "z-ai/glm-5.1":               (200000, 131072),
143:    # Sentinel.
144:    "minimax/minimax-m2":         (204800, 131072),
145:    # Judge — the qwen-400 victim. Context window is the binding constraint:
146:    # a large prompt + a generous max_tokens overran it -> HTTP-400.
147:    "qwen/qwen3.6-35b-a3b":       (131072, 65536),
148:}
149:
150:
240:def resolve_model_limits(model: str) -> Optional[tuple[int, Optional[int]]]:
241:    """Return (context_length, provider_completion_cap_or_None) for ``model``.
242:
243:    Resolution order: live /models table -> static fallback -> None.
244:    None means "unknown" -> the caller must PASS THROUGH the requested budget.
245:    """
246:    if not model:
247:        return None
248:    table = _get_models_cache()
249:    if model in table:
250:        return table[model]
251:    if model in _STATIC_MODEL_LIMITS:
252:        return _STATIC_MODEL_LIMITS[model]
253:    return None
254:
255:
256:def estimate_prompt_tokens(messages: list[dict]) -> int:
257:    """Cheap char/4 prompt-token estimate (no heavy tokenizer — §8.4).
258:
259:    Deliberately conservative-high: counts role + content chars so we slightly
260:    OVER-estimate the prompt, which makes the ceiling slightly SMALLER — the safe
261:    direction (a 400 is worse than a marginally shorter completion).
262:    """
263:    total_chars = 0
264:    for msg in messages or []:
265:        content = msg.get("content")
266:        if isinstance(content, str):
267:            total_chars += len(content)
268:        elif content is not None:
269:            total_chars += len(str(content))
270:        # small fixed overhead per message for role/framing tokens
271:        total_chars += 8
272:    return (total_chars // 4) + 1
273:
274:
275:def compute_allowed_max_tokens(
276:    model: str,
277:    prompt_tokens: int,
278:    requested_max_tokens: int,
279:    apply_completion_cap: bool = True,
280:) -> int:
281:    """Clamp ``requested_max_tokens`` so prompt + completion fits the context window.
282:
283:    Returns ``min(requested_max_tokens, provider_completion_cap,
284:                  context_length - prompt_tokens - safety_margin)``.
285:
286:    ``apply_completion_cap`` (default True): when False, the ``top_provider``
287:    completion-cap candidate is IGNORED and only the ``context_length`` bound
288:    applies. The caller sets this False for the PROVIDER-PINNED generator family
289:    (``_REASONING_FIRST_MODELS``): #1253 pins that family to full-cap providers
290:    (Novita/WandB >= 384k) and EXCLUDES DeepInfra, but OpenRouter's
291:    ``top_provider.max_completion_tokens`` is a popularity-ranked field that may
292:    report DeepInfra's 16384 — applying it would clamp a 32k-64k section request
293:    back down to 16384, re-introducing the exact #1253 starvation. The judge/
294:    verifier roles are NOT reasoning-first, so their completion-cap clamp (the
295:    actual HTTP-400 fix) stays intact (Codex/advisor B10 online-path finding).
296:
297:    CLAMP-DOWN-ONLY + PASS-THROUGH semantics:
298:      - resolver disabled OR model limits unknown  -> return requested unchanged.
299:      - the computed ceiling >= requested          -> return requested unchanged
300:        (the generator's full-cap budget never clamps).
301:      - the computed ceiling <  requested          -> return the ceiling, LOUD-log.
302:      - the computed ceiling <= 0 (prompt alone overruns the window) -> RAISE
303:        PromptTooLargeError. A tiny max_tokens could NOT make an over-window prompt
304:        well-formed (the request would still 400), so we surface the real cause
305:        loudly and deterministically rather than firing a doomed request or

589:def _relevance_scorer_mode() -> str:
590:    """Read `PG_RELEVANCE_SCORER` (default "lexical"). "semantic_v2" => the
591:    embedding-cosine scorer + restored relevance filter; anything else (incl.
592:    unset) => the legacy lexical `_row_relevance_facet` scorer, byte-identical."""
593:    return os.environ.get("PG_RELEVANCE_SCORER", "lexical").strip().lower()

596:def _semantic_scorer_enabled() -> bool:
597:    """True iff `PG_RELEVANCE_SCORER == semantic_v2`. Default OFF => byte-identical
598:    lexical selection (no embedder load, no filter change)."""
599:    return _relevance_scorer_mode() == "semantic_v2"
